revision.text:
{{infobox religious building | name = Sōma Nakamura Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Somanakamura shrine.jpg|270px]]
Haiden {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=270}} | location = 140 Nakamura, Kitamachi, Sōma City, Fukushima Prefecture, Japan | coordinates = {{coord|37|47|49.65|N|140|54|48.45|E|region:JP-07_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Sōma Nakamura Shrine}} | deity = [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]] | Shrine rank = | founded = 1611 (Keichō 16) | main hall = [[Nagare-zukuri]] | Other name = Myōken Nakamura Shrine | festival = April 18–19 | rituals = [[Sōma Nomaoi]] (July 23–25) | map = {{location map|Japan Fukushima Prefecture|label=Sōma Nakamura Shrine|float=center|relief=1|width=200|caption=}} }} '''Sōma Nakamura Shrine''' (相馬中村神社) is a Shinto shrine located in Nakamura, Sōma City, Fukushima Prefecture. It is also known as '''Myōken Nakamura Shrine''' (妙見中村神社) or simply Nakamura Shrine. The enshrined deity is {{ill|Ame-no-Minakanushi|ja|天之御中主神}} (identified with {{ill|Myōken Bosatsu|ja|妙見菩薩}}). The shrine serves as the starting point for the {{ill|Sōma Nomaoi|ja|相馬野馬追}} festival.{{Cite web|url=https://www.minyu-net.com/news/news/FM20211115-667668.php |title=Sōma Nakamura Shrine to Receive New Grand Torii After Earthquake Damage |publisher=Fukushima Minyu Newspaper |accessdate=2021-11-15}} == Overview == The origins of Sōma Nakamura Shrine trace back to the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}} when {{ill|Taira no Masakado|ja|平将門}}, the founder of the {{ill|Sōma clan|ja|相馬氏}}, established a Myōken Shrine in {{ill|Shimōsa Province|ja|下総国}} during the Jōhei era (931–937). As the Sōma clan moved to the Sōma district, a new shrine was established. By the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}} in the late 16th century, {{ill|Nakamura Castle (Mutsu Province)|ja|中村城 (陸奥国)}} became the clan's northern stronghold, governed by lords such as {{ill|Sōma Moritane|ja|相馬盛胤}} and {{ill|Sōma Takamine|ja|相馬隆胤}}. After the {{ill|Battle of Sekigahara|ja|関ヶ原の戦い}} in 1600, the Sōma clan was briefly dispossessed of their holdings but regained control in 1611, establishing the {{ill|Sōma Nakamura Domain|ja|相馬中村藩}}. The shrine was built by {{ill|Sōma Takatane|ja|相馬利胤}}, the first lord of the Nakamura Domain, near the southwest of Nakamura Castle in 1611. The current main shrine building was constructed in {{ill|1643|ja|寛永20年}} by {{ill|Sōma Yoshitane|ja|相馬義胤 (相馬中村藩主)}}, the second domain lord, and has been designated as a {{ill|National Important Cultural Property|ja|重要文化財}}. During the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離}} led to the removal of Myōken Bosatsu, and the shrine was renamed Sōma Nakamura Shrine. Another shrine, {{ill|Sōma Shrine (Sōma City)|lt=Sōma Shrine|ja|相馬神社 (相馬市)}}, was established in 1880 on the ruins of Nakamura Castle's main keep after the {{ill|Boshin War|ja|戊辰戦争}}. The shrine has been under the care of the Tashiro family, who have served as hereditary priests since its establishment. The shrine formerly maintained stables for horses, including retired racehorses such as {{ill|Basikeen|ja|バシケーン}} (winner of the 2010 Nakayama Grand Jump) and {{ill|Million Disc|ja|ミリオンディスク}} (winner of the 2009 Capella Stakes). However, the stables were dismantled around 2019.[https://news.netkeiba.com/?pid=news_view&no=72447 Basikeen Finds a New Home at Sōma Nakamura Shrine] == Cultural Properties == '''Important Cultural Properties''' * Main Shrine, Worship Hall, and Offering Hall (1 building) ''' Includes a "kuden" (宮殿) altar. == images == File:Soma otemon.JPG|{{ill|Nakamura Castle (Mutsu Province)|ja|中村城 (陸奥国)}} Otemon Gate (2013) File:Nakamura shrine 003.JPG|Entrance to Nakamura Shrine, featuring a horse statue associated with {{ill|Sōma Nomaoi|ja|相馬野馬追}} (2013) == See Also == * {{ill|Nakamura Castle (Mutsu Province)|ja|中村城 (陸奥国)}} * {{ill|Sōma Nomaoi|ja|相馬野馬追}} == External Links == * [https://soumanakamurajinja.com/ Sōma Nakamura Shrine Official Website] * [https://ameblo.jp/umatoayumu-soma/ Uma to Haiden {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=270}} | location = 140 Nakamura, Kitamachi, Sōma City, Fukushima Prefecture, Japan | coordinates = {{coord|37|47|49.65|N|140|54|48.45|E|region:JP-07_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Sōma Nakamura Shrine}} | deity = [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]] | Shrine rank = | founded = 1611 (Keichō 16) | main hall = [[Nagare-zukuri]] | Other name = Myōken Nakamura Shrine | festival = April 18–19 | rituals = [[Sōma Nomaoi]] (July 23–25) | map = {{location map|Japan Fukushima Prefecture|label=Sōma Nakamura Shrine|float=center|relief=1|width=200|caption=}} }} '''Sōma Nakamura Shrine''' (相馬中村神社) is a Shinto shrine located in Nakamura, Sōma City, Fukushima Prefecture. It is also known as '''Myōken Nakamura Shrine''' (妙見中村神社) or simply Nakamura Shrine. The enshrined deity is {{ill|Ame-no-Minakanushi|ja|天之御中主神}} (identified with {{ill|Myōken Bosatsu|ja|妙見菩薩}}). The shrine serves as the starting point for the {{ill|Sōma Nomaoi|ja|相馬野馬追}} festival.{{Cite web|url=https://www.minyu-net.com/news/news/FM20211115-667668.php |title=Sōma Nakamura Shrine to Receive New Grand Torii After Earthquake Damage |publisher=Fukushima Minyu Newspaper |accessdate=2021-11-15}} == Overview == The origins of Sōma Nakamura Shrine trace back to the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}} when {{ill|Taira no Masakado|ja|平将門}}, the founder of the {{ill|Sōma clan|ja|相馬氏}}, established a Myōken Shrine in {{ill|Shimōsa Province|ja|下総国}} during the Jōhei era (931–937). As the Sōma clan moved to the Sōma district, a new shrine was established. By the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}} in the late 16th century, {{ill|Nakamura Castle (Mutsu Province)|ja|中村城 (陸奥国)}} became the clan's northern stronghold, governed by lords such as {{ill|Sōma Moritane|ja|相馬盛胤}} and {{ill|Sōma Takamine|ja|相馬隆胤}}. After the {{ill|Battle of Sekigahara|ja|関ヶ原の戦い}} in 1600, the Sōma clan was briefly dispossessed of their holdings but regained control in 1611, establishing the {{ill|Sōma Nakamura Domain|ja|相馬中村藩}}. The shrine was built by {{ill|Sōma Takatane|ja|相馬利胤}}, the first lord of the Nakamura Domain, near the southwest of Nakamura Castle in 1611. The current main shrine building was constructed in {{ill|1643|ja|寛永20年}} by {{ill|Sōma Yoshitane|ja|相馬義胤 (相馬中村藩主)}}, the second domain lord, and has been designated as a {{ill|National Important Cultural Property|ja|重要文化財}}. During the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離}} led to the removal of Myōken Bosatsu, and the shrine was renamed Sōma Nakamura Shrine. Another shrine, {{ill|Sōma Shrine (Sōma City)|lt=Sōma Shrine|ja|相馬神社 (相馬市)}}, was established in 1880 on the ruins of Nakamura Castle's main keep after the {{ill|Boshin War|ja|戊辰戦争}}. The shrine has been under the care of the Tashiro family, who have served as hereditary priests since its establishment. The shrine formerly maintained stables for horses, including retired racehorses such as {{ill|Basikeen|ja|バシケーン}} (winner of the 2010 Nakayama Grand Jump) and {{ill|Million Disc|ja|ミリオンディスク}} (winner of the 2009 Capella Stakes). However, the stables were dismantled around 2019.[https://news.netkeiba.com/?pid=news_view&no=72447 Basikeen Finds a New Home at Sōma Nakamura Shrine] == Cultural Properties == '''Important Cultural Properties''' * Main Shrine, Worship Hall, and Offering Hall (1 building) ''' Includes a "kuden" (宮殿) altar. == images == File:Soma otemon.JPG|{{ill|Nakamura Castle (Mutsu Province)|ja|中村城 (陸奥国)}} revision.text:
{{infobox religious building | name = Sōma Nakamura Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Somanakamura shrine.jpg|270px]]
Haiden {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=270}} | location = 140 Nakamura, Kitamachi, Sōma City, Fukushima Prefecture, Japan | coordinates = {{coord|37|47|49.65|N|140|54|48.45|E|region:JP-07_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Sōma Nakamura Shrine}} | deity = [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]] | Shrine rank = | founded = 1611 (Keichō 16) | main hall = [[Nagare-zukuri]] | Other name = Myōken Nakamura Shrine | festival = April 18–19 | rituals = [[Sōma Nomaoi]] (July 23–25) | map = {{location map|Japan Fukushima Prefecture|label=Sōma Nakamura Shrine|float=center|relief=1|width=200|caption=}} }} '''Sōma Nakamura Shrine''' (相馬中村神社) is a Shinto shrine located in Nakamura, Sōma City, Fukushima Prefecture. It is also known as '''Myōken Nakamura Shrine''' (妙見中村神社) or simply Nakamura Shrine. The enshrined deity is {{ill|Ame-no-Minakanushi|ja|天之御中主神}} (identified with {{ill|Myōken Bosatsu|ja|妙見菩薩}}). The shrine serves as the starting point for the {{ill|Sōma Nomaoi|ja|相馬野馬追}} festival.{{Cite web|url=https://www.minyu-net.com/news/news/FM20211115-667668.php |title=Sōma Nakamura Shrine to Receive New Grand Torii After Earthquake Damage |publisher=Fukushima Minyu Newspaper |accessdate=2021-11-15}} == Overview == The origins of Sōma Nakamura Shrine trace back to the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}} when {{ill|Taira no Masakado|ja|平将門}}, the founder of the {{ill|Sōma clan|ja|相馬氏}}, established a Myōken Shrine in {{ill|Shimōsa Province|ja|下総国}} during the Jōhei era (931–937). As the Sōma clan moved to the Sōma district, a new shrine was established. By the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}} in the late 16th century, {{ill|Nakamura Castle (Mutsu Province)|ja|中村城 (陸奥国)}} became the clan's northern stronghold, governed by lords such as {{ill|Sōma Moritane|ja|相馬盛胤}} and {{ill|Sōma Takamine|ja|相馬隆胤}}. After the {{ill|Battle of Sekigahara|ja|関ヶ原の戦い}} in 1600, the Sōma clan was briefly dispossessed of their holdings but regained control in 1611, establishing the {{ill|Sōma Nakamura Domain|ja|相馬中村藩}}. The shrine was built by {{ill|Sōma Takatane|ja|相馬利胤}}, the first lord of the Nakamura Domain, near the southwest of Nakamura Castle in 1611. The current main shrine building was constructed in {{ill|1643|ja|寛永20年}} by {{ill|Sōma Yoshitane|ja|相馬義胤 (相馬中村藩主)}}, the second domain lord, and has been designated as a {{ill|National Important Cultural Property|ja|重要文化財}}. During the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離}} led to the removal of Myōken Bosatsu, and the shrine was renamed Sōma Nakamura Shrine. Another shrine, {{ill|Sōma Shrine (Sōma City)|lt=Sōma Shrine|ja|相馬神社 (相馬市)}}, was established in 1880 on the ruins of Nakamura Castle's main keep after the {{ill|Boshin War|ja|戊辰戦争}}. The shrine has been under the care of the Tashiro family, who have served as hereditary priests since its establishment. The shrine formerly maintained stables for horses, including retired racehorses such as {{ill|Basikeen|ja|バシケーン}} (winner of the 2010 Nakayama Grand Jump) and {{ill|Million Disc|ja|ミリオンディスク}} (winner of the 2009 Capella Stakes). However, the stables were dismantled around 2019.[https://news.netkeiba.com/?pid=news_view&no=72447 Basikeen Finds a New Home at Sōma Nakamura Shrine] == Cultural Properties == '''Important Cultural Properties''' * Main Shrine, Worship Hall, and Offering Hall (1 building) ''' Includes a "kuden" (宮殿) altar. == images == File:Soma otemon.JPG|{{ill|Nakamura Castle (Mutsu Province)|ja|中村城 (陸奥国)}} Otemon Gate (2013) File:Nakamura shrine 003.JPG|Entrance to Nakamura Shrine, featuring a horse statue associated with {{ill|Sōma Nomaoi|ja|相馬野馬追}} (2013) == See Also == * {{ill|Nakamura Castle (Mutsu Province)|ja|中村城 (陸奥国)}} * {{ill|Sōma Nomaoi|ja|相馬野馬追}} == External Links == * [https://soumanakamurajinja.com/ Sōma Nakamura Shrine Official Website] * [https://ameblo.jp/umatoayumu-soma/ Uma to Ayumu SOMA Blog] ==References== {{reflist}} {{神道 横}} [[Category:completion tier 2]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Sōma Nakamura Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Sōma Nakamura Shrine]][[Category:qq]] {{infobox religious building | name = Sōma Nakamura Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Somanakamura shrine.jpg|270px]]
Haiden {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=270}} | location = 140 Nakamura, Kitamachi, Sōma City, Fukushima Prefecture, Japan | coordinates = {{coord|37|47|49.65|N|140|54|48.45|E|region:JP-07_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Sōma Nakamura Shrine}} | deity = [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]] | Shrine rank = | founded = 1611 (Keichō 16) | main hall = [[Nagare-zukuri]] | Other name = Myōken Nakamura Shrine | festival = April 18–19 | rituals = [[Sōma Nomaoi]] (July 23–25) | map = {{location map|Japan Fukushima Prefecture|label=Sōma Nakamura Shrine|float=center|relief=1|width=200|caption=}} }} '''Sōma Nakamura Shrine''' (相馬中村神社) is a Shinto shrine located in Nakamura, Sōma City, Fukushima Prefecture. It is also known as '''Myōken Nakamura Shrine''' (妙見中村神社) or simply Nakamura Shrine. The enshrined deity is {{ill|Ame-no-Minakanushi|ja|天之御中主神}} (identified with {{ill|Myōken Bosatsu|ja|妙見菩薩}}). The shrine serves as the starting point for the {{ill|Sōma Nomaoi|ja|相馬野馬追}} festival.{{Cite web|url=https://www.minyu-net.com/news/news/FM20211115-667668.php |title=Sōma Nakamura Shrine to Receive New Grand Torii After Earthquake Damage |publisher=Fukushima Minyu Newspaper |accessdate=2021-11-15}} == Overview == The origins of Sōma Nakamura Shrine trace back to the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}} when {{ill|Taira no Masakado|ja|平将門}}, the founder of the {{ill|Sōma clan|ja|相馬氏}}, established a Myōken Shrine in {{ill|Shimōsa Province|ja|下総国}} during the Jōhei era (931–937). As the Sōma clan moved to the Sōma district, a new shrine was established. By the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}} in the late 16th century, {{ill|Nakamura Castle (Mutsu Province)|ja|中村城 (陸奥国)}} became the clan's northern stronghold, governed by lords such as {{ill|Sōma Moritane|ja|相馬盛胤}} and {{ill|Sōma Takamine|ja|相馬隆胤}}. After the {{ill|Battle of Sekigahara|ja|関ヶ原の戦い}} in 1600, the Sōma clan was briefly dispossessed of their holdings but regained control in 1611, establishing the {{ill|Sōma Nakamura Domain|ja|相馬中村藩}}. The shrine was built by {{ill|Sōma Takatane|ja|相馬利胤}}, the first lord of the Nakamura Domain, near the southwest of Nakamura Castle in 1611. The current main shrine building was constructed in {{ill|1643|ja|寛永20年}} by {{ill|Sōma Yoshitane|ja|相馬義胤 (相馬中村藩主)}}, the second domain lord, and has been designated as a {{ill|National Important Cultural Property|ja|重要文化財}}. During the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離}} led to the removal of Myōken Bosatsu, and the shrine was renamed Sōma Nakamura Shrine. Another shrine, {{ill|Sōma Shrine (Sōma City)|lt=Sōma Shrine|ja|相馬神社 (相馬市)}}, was established in 1880 on the ruins of Nakamura Castle's main keep after the {{ill|Boshin War|ja|戊辰戦争}}. The shrine has been under the care of the Tashiro family, who have served as hereditary priests since its establishment. The shrine formerly maintained stables for horses, including retired racehorses such as {{ill|Basikeen|ja|バシケーン}} (winner of the 2010 Nakayama Grand Jump) and {{ill|Million Disc|ja|ミリオンディスク}} (winner of the 2009 Capella Stakes). However, the stables were dismantled around 2019.[https://news.netkeiba.com/?pid=news_view&no=72447 Basikeen Finds a New Home at Sōma Nakamura Shrine] == Cultural Properties == '''Important Cultural Properties''' * Main Shrine, Worship Hall, and Offering Hall (1 building) ''' Includes a "kuden" (宮殿) altar. == images == File:Soma otemon.JPG|{{ill|Nakamura Castle (Mutsu Province)|ja|中村城 (陸奥国)}} Otemon Gate (2013) File:Nakamura shrine 003.JPG|Entrance to Nakamura Shrine, featuring a horse statue associated with {{ill|Sōma Nomaoi|ja|相馬野馬追}} (2013) == See Also == * {{ill|Nakamura Castle (Mutsu Province)|ja|中村城 (陸奥国)}} * {{ill|Sōma Nomaoi|ja|相馬野馬追}} == External Links == * [https://soumanakamurajinja.com/ Sōma Nakamura Shrine Official Website] * [https://ameblo.jp/umatoayumu-soma/ Uma to {{infobox religious building | name = Sōma Nakamura Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Somanakamura shrine.jpg|270px]]
Haiden {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=270}} | location = 140 Nakamura, Kitamachi, Sōma City, Fukushima Prefecture, Japan | coordinates = {{coord|37|47|49.65|N|140|54|48.45|E|region:JP-07_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Sōma Nakamura Shrine}} | deity = [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]] | Shrine rank = | founded = 1611 (Keichō 16) | main hall = [[Nagare-zukuri]] | Other name = Myōken Nakamura Shrine | festival = April 18–19 | rituals = [[Sōma Nomaoi]] (July 23–25) | map = {{location map|Japan Fukushima Prefecture|label=Sōma Nakamura Shrine|float=center|relief=1|width=200|caption=}} }} '''Sōma Nakamura Shrine''' (相馬中村神社) is a Shinto shrine located in Nakamura, Sōma City, Fukushima Prefecture. It is also known as '''Myōken Nakamura Shrine''' (妙見中村神社) or simply Nakamura Shrine. The enshrined deity is {{ill|Ame-no-Minakanushi|ja|天之御中主神}} (identified with {{ill|Myōken Bosatsu|ja|妙見菩薩}}). The shrine serves as the starting point for the {{ill|Sōma Nomaoi|ja|相馬野馬追}} festival.{{Cite web|url=https://www.minyu-net.com/news/news/FM20211115-667668.php |title=Sōma Nakamura Shrine to Receive New Grand Torii After Earthquake Damage |publisher=Fukushima Minyu Newspaper |accessdate=2021-11-15}} == Overview == The origins of Sōma Nakamura Shrine trace back to the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}} when {{ill|Taira no Masakado|ja|平将門}}, the founder of the {{ill|Sōma clan|ja|相馬氏}}, established a Myōken Shrine in {{ill|Shimōsa Province|ja|下総国}} during the Jōhei era (931–937). As the Sōma clan moved to the Sōma district, a new shrine was established. By the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}} in the late 16th century, {{ill|Nakamura Castle (Mutsu Province)|ja|中村城 (陸奥国)}} became the clan's northern stronghold, governed by lords such as {{ill|Sōma Moritane|ja|相馬盛胤}} and {{ill|Sōma Takamine|ja|相馬隆胤}}. After the {{ill|Battle of Sekigahara|ja|関ヶ原の戦い}} in 1600, the Sōma clan was briefly dispossessed of their holdings but regained control in 1611, establishing the {{ill|Sōma Nakamura Domain|ja|相馬中村藩}}. The shrine was built by {{ill|Sōma Takatane|ja|相馬利胤}}, the first lord of the Nakamura Domain, near the southwest of Nakamura Castle in 1611. The current main shrine building was constructed in {{ill|1643|ja|寛永20年}} by {{ill|Sōma Yoshitane|ja|相馬義胤 (相馬中村藩主)}}, the second domain lord, and has been designated as a {{ill|National Important Cultural Property|ja|重要文化財}}. During the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離}} led to the removal of Myōken Bosatsu, and the shrine was renamed Sōma Nakamura Shrine. Another shrine, {{ill|Sōma Shrine (Sōma City)|lt=Sōma Shrine|ja|相馬神社 (相馬市)}}, was established in 1880 on the ruins of Nakamura Castle's main keep after the {{ill|Boshin War|ja|戊辰戦争}}. The shrine has been under the care of the Tashiro family, who have served as hereditary priests since its establishment. The shrine formerly maintained stables for horses, including retired racehorses such as {{ill|Basikeen|ja|バシケーン}} (winner of the 2010 Nakayama Grand Jump) and {{ill|Million Disc|ja|ミリオンディスク}} (winner of the 2009 Capella Stakes). However, the stables were dismantled around 2019.[https://news.netkeiba.com/?pid=news_view&no=72447 Basikeen Finds a New Home at Sōma Nakamura Shrine] == Cultural Properties == '''Important Cultural Properties''' * Main Shrine, Worship Hall, and Offering Hall (1 building) ''' Includes a "kuden" (宮殿) altar. == images == File:Soma otemon.JPG|{{ill|Nakamura Castle (Mutsu Province)|ja|中村城 (陸奥国)}} revision.text:
{{infobox religious building | name = Sōma Nakamura Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Somanakamura shrine.jpg|270px]]
Haiden {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=270}} | location = 140 Nakamura, Kitamachi, Sōma City, Fukushima Prefecture, Japan | coordinates = {{coord|37|47|49.65|N|140|54|48.45|E|region:JP-07_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Sōma Nakamura Shrine}} | deity = [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]] | Shrine rank = | founded = 1611 (Keichō 16) | main hall = [[Nagare-zukuri]] | Other name = Myōken Nakamura Shrine | festival = April 18–19 | rituals = [[Sōma Nomaoi]] (July 23–25) | map = {{location map|Japan Fukushima Prefecture|label=Sōma Nakamura Shrine|float=center|relief=1|width=200|caption=}} }} '''Sōma Nakamura Shrine''' (相馬中村神社) is a Shinto shrine located in Nakamura, Sōma City, Fukushima Prefecture. It is also known as '''Myōken Nakamura Shrine''' (妙見中村神社) or simply Nakamura Shrine. The enshrined deity is {{ill|Ame-no-Minakanushi|ja|天之御中主神}} (identified with {{ill|Myōken Bosatsu|ja|妙見菩薩}}). The shrine serves as the starting point for the {{ill|Sōma Nomaoi|ja|相馬野馬追}} festival.{{Cite web|url=https://www.minyu-net.com/news/news/FM20211115-667668.php |title=Sōma Nakamura Shrine to Receive New Grand Torii After Earthquake Damage |publisher=Fukushima Minyu Newspaper |accessdate=2021-11-15}} == Overview == The origins of Sōma Nakamura Shrine trace back to the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}} when {{ill|Taira no Masakado|ja|平将門}}, the founder of the {{ill|Sōma clan|ja|相馬氏}}, established a Myōken Shrine in {{ill|Shimōsa Province|ja|下総国}} during the Jōhei era (931–937). As the Sōma clan moved to the Sōma district, a new shrine was established. By the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}} in the late 16th century, {{ill|Nakamura Castle (Mutsu Province)|ja|中村城 (Mutsu Province )}} became the clan's northern stronghold, governed by lords such as {{ill|Sōma Moritane|ja|相馬盛胤}} and {{ill|Sōma Takamine|ja|相馬隆胤}}. After the {{ill|Battle of Sekigahara|ja|関ヶ原の戦い}} in 1600, the Sōma clan was briefly dispossessed of their holdings but regained control in 1611, establishing the {{ill|Sōma Nakamura Domain|ja|相馬中村藩}}. The shrine was built by {{ill|Sōma Takatane|ja|相馬利胤}}, the first lord of the Nakamura Domain, near the southwest of Nakamura Castle in 1611. The current main shrine building was constructed in {{ill|1643|ja|寛永20年}} by {{ill|Sōma Yoshitane|ja|相馬義胤 (相馬中村藩主)}}, the second domain lord, and has been designated as a {{ill|National Important Cultural Property|ja|重要文化財}}. During the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離}} led to the removal of Myōken Bosatsu, and the shrine was renamed Sōma Nakamura Shrine. Another shrine, {{ill|Sōma Shrine (Sōma City)|lt=Sōma Shrine|ja|相馬神社 (相馬市)}}, was established in 1880 on the ruins of Nakamura Castle's main keep after the {{ill|Boshin War|ja|戊辰戦争}}. The shrine has been under the care of the Tashiro family, who have served as hereditary priests since its establishment. The shrine formerly maintained stables for horses, including retired racehorses such as {{ill|Basikeen|ja|バシケーン}} (winner of the 2010 Nakayama Grand Jump) and {{ill|Million Disc|ja|ミリオンディスク}} (winner of the 2009 Capella Stakes). However, the stables were dismantled around 2019.[https://news.netkeiba.com/?pid=news_view&no=72447 Basikeen Finds a New Home at Sōma Nakamura Shrine] == Cultural Properties == '''Important Cultural Properties''' * Main Shrine, Worship Hall, and Offering Hall (1 building) ''' Includes a "kuden" (宮殿) altar. == images == File:Soma otemon.JPG|{{ill|Nakamura Castle (Mutsu Province)|ja|中村城 (Mutsu Province )}} Otemon Gate (2013) File:Nakamura shrine 003.JPG|Entrance to Nakamura Shrine, featuring a horse statue associated with {{ill|Sōma Nomaoi|ja|相馬野馬追}} (2013) == See Also == * {{ill|Nakamura Castle (Mutsu Province)|ja|中村城 (Mutsu Province )}} * {{ill|Sōma Nomaoi|ja|相馬野馬追}} == External Links == * [https://soumanakamurajinja.com/ Sōma Nakamura Shrine Official Website] * {{infobox religious building | name = Sōma Nakamura Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Somanakamura shrine.jpg|270px]]
Haiden {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=270}} | location = 140 Nakamura, Kitamachi, Sōma City, Fukushima Prefecture, Japan | coordinates = {{coord|37|47|49.65|N|140|54|48.45|E|region:JP-07_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Sōma Nakamura Shrine}} | deity = [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]] | Shrine rank = | founded = 1611 (Keichō 16) | main hall = [[Nagare-zukuri]] | Other name = Myōken Nakamura Shrine | festival = April 18–19 | rituals = [[Sōma Nomaoi]] (July 23–25) | map = {{location map|Japan Fukushima Prefecture|label=Sōma Nakamura Shrine|float=center|relief=1|width=200|caption=}} }} '''Sōma Nakamura Shrine''' (相馬中村神社) is a Shinto shrine located in Nakamura, Sōma City, Fukushima Prefecture. It is also known as '''Myōken Nakamura Shrine''' (妙見中村神社) or simply Nakamura Shrine. The enshrined deity is {{ill|Ame-no-Minakanushi|ja|天之御中主神}} (identified with {{ill|Myōken Bosatsu|ja|妙見菩薩}}). The shrine serves as the starting point for the {{ill|Sōma Nomaoi|ja|相馬野馬追}} festival.{{Cite web|url=https://www.minyu-net.com/news/news/FM20211115-667668.php |title=Sōma Nakamura Shrine to Receive New Grand Torii After Earthquake Damage |publisher=Fukushima Minyu Newspaper |accessdate=2021-11-15}} == Overview == The origins of Sōma Nakamura Shrine trace back to the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}} when {{ill|Taira no Masakado|ja|平将門}}, the founder of the {{ill|Sōma clan|ja|相馬氏}}, established a Myōken Shrine in {{ill|Shimōsa Province|ja|下総国}} during the Jōhei era (931–937). As the Sōma clan moved to the Sōma district, a new shrine was established. By the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}} in the late 16th century, {{ill|Nakamura Castle (Mutsu Province)|ja|中村城 (Mutsu Province )}} became the clan's northern stronghold, governed by lords such as {{ill|Sōma Moritane|ja|相馬盛胤}} and {{ill|Sōma Takamine|ja|相馬隆胤}}. After the {{ill|Battle of Sekigahara|ja|関ヶ原の戦い}} in 1600, the Sōma clan was briefly dispossessed of their holdings but regained control in 1611, establishing the {{ill|Sōma Nakamura Domain|ja|相馬中村藩}}. The shrine was built by {{ill|Sōma Takatane|ja|相馬利胤}}, the first lord of the Nakamura Domain, near the southwest of Nakamura Castle in 1611. The current main shrine building was constructed in {{ill|1643|ja|寛永20年}} by {{ill|Sōma Yoshitane|ja|相馬義胤 (相馬中村藩主)}}, the second domain lord, and has been designated as a {{ill|National Important Cultural Property|ja|重要文化財}}. During the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離}} led to the removal of Myōken Bosatsu, and the shrine was renamed Sōma Nakamura Shrine. Another shrine, {{ill|Sōma Shrine (Sōma City)|lt=Sōma Shrine|ja|相馬神社 (相馬市)}}, was established in 1880 on the ruins of Nakamura Castle's main keep after the {{ill|Boshin War|ja|戊辰戦争}}. The shrine has been under the care of the Tashiro family, who have served as hereditary priests since its establishment. The shrine formerly maintained stables for horses, including retired racehorses such as {{ill|Basikeen|ja|バシケーン}} (winner of the 2010 Nakayama Grand Jump) and {{ill|Million Disc|ja|ミリオンディスク}} (winner of the 2009 Capella Stakes). However, the stables were dismantled around 2019.[https://news.netkeiba.com/?pid=news_view&no=72447 Basikeen Finds a New Home at Sōma Nakamura Shrine] == Cultural Properties == '''Important Cultural Properties''' * Main Shrine, Worship Hall, and Offering Hall (1 building) ''' Includes a "kuden" (宮殿) altar. == images == File:Soma otemon.JPG|{{ill|Nakamura Castle (Mutsu Province)|ja|中村城 (Mutsu Province )}} Otemon Gate (2013) File:Nakamura shrine 003.JPG|Entrance to Nakamura Shrine, featuring a horse statue associated with {{ill|Sōma Nomaoi|ja|相馬野馬追}} (2013) == See Also == * {{ill|Nakamura revision.comment: Text replacement - "qq" to "11"
revision.text:
{{infobox religious building | name = Sōma Nakamura Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Somanakamura shrine.jpg|270px]]
Haiden {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=270}} | location = 140 Nakamura, Kitamachi, Sōma City, Fukushima Prefecture, Japan | coordinates = {{coord|37|47|49.65|N|140|54|48.45|E|region:JP-07_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Sōma Nakamura Shrine}} | deity = [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]] | Shrine rank = | founded = 1611 (Keichō 16) | main hall = [[Nagare-zukuri]] | Other name = Myōken Nakamura Shrine | festival = April 18–19 | rituals = [[Sōma Nomaoi]] (July 23–25) | map = {{location map|Japan Fukushima Prefecture|label=Sōma Nakamura Shrine|float=center|relief=1|width=200|caption=}} }} '''Sōma Nakamura Shrine''' (相馬中村神社) is a Shinto shrine located in Nakamura, Sōma City, Fukushima Prefecture. It is also known as '''Myōken Nakamura Shrine''' (妙見中村神社) or simply Nakamura Shrine. The enshrined deity is {{ill|Ame-no-Minakanushi|ja|天之御中主神}} (identified with {{ill|Myōken Bosatsu|ja|妙見菩薩}}). The shrine serves as the starting point for the {{ill|Sōma Nomaoi|ja|相馬野馬追}} festival.{{Cite web|url=https://www.minyu-net.com/news/news/FM20211115-667668.php |title=Sōma Nakamura Shrine to Receive New Grand Torii After Earthquake Damage |publisher=Fukushima Minyu Newspaper |accessdate=2021-11-15}} == Overview == The origins of Sōma Nakamura Shrine trace back to the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}} when {{ill|Taira no Masakado|ja|平将門}}, the founder of the {{ill|Sōma clan|ja|相馬氏}}, established a Myōken Shrine in {{ill|Shimōsa Province|ja|下総国}} during the Jōhei era (931–937). As the Sōma clan moved to the Sōma district, a new shrine was established. By the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}} in the late 16th century, {{ill|Nakamura Castle (Mutsu Province)|ja|中村城 (Mutsu Province )}} became the clan's northern stronghold, governed by lords such as {{ill|Sōma Moritane|ja|相馬盛胤}} and {{ill|Sōma Takamine|ja|相馬隆胤}}. After the {{ill|Battle of Sekigahara|ja|関ヶ原の戦い}} in 1600, the Sōma clan was briefly dispossessed of their holdings but regained control in 1611, establishing the {{ill|Sōma Nakamura Domain|ja|相馬中村藩}}. The shrine was built by {{ill|Sōma Takatane|ja|相馬利胤}}, the first lord of the Nakamura Domain, near the southwest of Nakamura Castle in 1611. The current main shrine building was constructed in {{ill|1643|ja|寛永20年}} by {{ill|Sōma Yoshitane|ja|相馬義胤 (相馬中村藩主)}}, the second domain lord, and has been designated as a {{ill|National Important Cultural Property|ja|重要文化財}}. During the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離}} led to the removal of Myōken Bosatsu, and the shrine was renamed Sōma Nakamura Shrine. Another shrine, {{ill|Sōma Shrine (Sōma City)|lt=Sōma Shrine|ja|相馬神社 (相馬市)}}, was established in 1880 on the ruins of Nakamura Castle's main keep after the {{ill|Boshin War|ja|戊辰戦争}}. The shrine has been under the care of the Tashiro family, who have served as hereditary priests since its establishment. The shrine formerly maintained stables for horses, including retired racehorses such as {{ill|Basikeen|ja|バシケーン}} (winner of the 2010 Nakayama Grand Jump) and {{ill|Million Disc|ja|ミリオンディスク}} (winner of the 2009 Capella Stakes). However, the stables were dismantled around 2019.[https://news.netkeiba.com/?pid=news_view&no=72447 Basikeen Finds a New Home at Sōma Nakamura Shrine] == Cultural Properties == '''Important Cultural Properties''' * Main Shrine, Worship Hall, and Offering Hall (1 building) ''' Includes a "kuden" (宮殿) altar. == images == File:Soma otemon.JPG|{{ill|Nakamura Castle (Mutsu Province)|ja|中村城 (Mutsu Province )}} Otemon Gate (2013) File:Nakamura shrine 003.JPG|Entrance to Nakamura Shrine, featuring a horse statue associated with {{ill|Sōma Nomaoi|ja|相馬野馬追}} (2013) == See Also == * {{ill|Nakamura Castle (Mutsu Province)|ja|中村城 (Mutsu Province )}} * {{ill|Sōma Nomaoi|ja|相馬野馬追}} == External Links == * [https://soumanakamurajinja.com/ Sōma Nakamura Shrine Official Website] * [https://ameblo.jp/umatoayumu-soma/ Uma to Ayumu SOMA Blog] ==References== {{reflist}} {{神道 横}} [[Category:completion tier 2]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Sōma Nakamura Shrine]][[Category:11]] revision.text:
{{infobox religious building | name = Sōma Nakamura Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Somanakamura shrine.jpg|270px]]
Haiden {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=270}} | location = 140 Nakamura, Kitamachi, Sōma City, Fukushima Prefecture, Japan | coordinates = {{coord|37|47|49.65|N|140|54|48.45|E|region:JP-07_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Sōma Nakamura Shrine}} | deity = [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]] | Shrine rank = | founded = 1611 (Keichō 16) | main hall = [[Nagare-zukuri]] | Other name = Myōken Nakamura Shrine | festival = April 18–19 | rituals = [[Sōma Nomaoi]] (July 23–25) | map = {{location map|Japan Fukushima Prefecture|label=Sōma Nakamura Shrine|float=center|relief=1|width=200|caption=}} }} '''Sōma Nakamura Shrine''' (相馬中村神社) is a Shinto shrine located in Nakamura, Sōma City, Fukushima Prefecture. It is also known as '''Myōken Nakamura Shrine''' (妙見中村神社) or simply Nakamura Shrine. The enshrined deity is {{ill|Ame-no-Minakanushi|ja|天之御中主神}} (identified with {{ill|Myōken Bosatsu|ja|妙見菩薩}}). The shrine serves as the starting point for the {{ill|Sōma Nomaoi|ja|相馬野馬追}} festival.{{Cite web|url=https://www.minyu-net.com/news/news/FM20211115-667668.php |title=Sōma Nakamura Shrine to Receive New Grand Torii After Earthquake Damage |publisher=Fukushima Minyu Newspaper |accessdate=2021-11-15}} == Overview == The origins of Sōma Nakamura Shrine trace back to the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}} when {{ill|Taira no Masakado|ja|平将門|1=Taira no Masakado}}, the founder of the {{ill|Sōma clan|ja|相馬氏}}, established a Myōken Shrine in {{ill|Shimōsa Province|ja|下総国}} during the Jōhei era (931–937). As the Sōma clan moved to the Sōma district, a new shrine was established. By the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}} in the late 16th century, {{ill|Nakamura Castle (Mutsu Province)|ja|中村城 (Mutsu Province )}} became the clan's northern stronghold, governed by lords such as {{ill|Sōma Moritane|ja|相馬盛胤}} and {{ill|Sōma Takamine|ja|相馬隆胤}}. After the {{ill|Battle of Sekigahara|ja|関ヶ原の戦い}} in 1600, the Sōma clan was briefly dispossessed of their holdings but regained control in 1611, establishing the {{ill|Sōma Nakamura Domain|ja|相馬中村藩}}. The shrine was built by {{ill|Sōma Takatane|ja|相馬利胤}}, the first lord of the Nakamura Domain, near the southwest of Nakamura Castle in 1611. The current main shrine building was constructed in {{ill|1643|ja|寛永20年}} by {{ill|Sōma Yoshitane|ja|相馬義胤 (相馬中村藩主)}}, the second domain lord, and has been designated as a {{ill|National Important Cultural Property|ja|重要文化財}}. During the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離}} led to the removal of Myōken Bosatsu, and the shrine was renamed Sōma Nakamura Shrine. Another shrine, {{ill|Sōma Shrine (Sōma City)|lt=Sōma Shrine|ja|相馬神社 (相馬市)}}, was established in 1880 on the ruins of Nakamura Castle's main keep after the {{ill|Boshin War|ja|戊辰戦争}}. The shrine has been under the care of the Tashiro family, who have served as hereditary priests since its establishment. The shrine formerly maintained stables for horses, including retired racehorses such as {{ill|Basikeen|ja|バシケーン}} (winner of the 2010 Nakayama Grand Jump) and {{ill|Million Disc|ja|ミリオンディスク}} (winner of the 2009 Capella Stakes). However, the stables were dismantled around 2019.[https://news.netkeiba.com/?pid=news_view&no=72447 Basikeen Finds a New Home at Sōma Nakamura Shrine] == Cultural Properties == '''Important Cultural Properties''' * Main Shrine, Worship Hall, and Offering Hall (1 building) ''' Includes a "kuden" (宮殿) altar. == images == File:Soma otemon.JPG|{{ill|Nakamura Castle (Mutsu Province)|ja|中村城 (Mutsu Province )}} Otemon Gate (2013) File:Nakamura shrine 003.JPG|Entrance to Nakamura Shrine, featuring a horse statue associated with {{ill|Sōma Nomaoi|ja|相馬野馬追}} (2013) == See Also == * {{ill|Nakamura Castle (Mutsu Province)|ja|中村城 (Mutsu Province )}} * {{ill|Sōma Nomaoi|ja|相馬野馬追}} == External Links == * [https://soumanakamurajinja.com/ Sōma Nakamura Shrine Official Website] * {{infobox religious building | name = Sōma Nakamura Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Somanakamura shrine.jpg|270px]]
Haiden {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=270}} | location = 140 Nakamura, Kitamachi, Sōma City, Fukushima Prefecture, Japan | coordinates = {{coord|37|47|49.65|N|140|54|48.45|E|region:JP-07_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Sōma Nakamura Shrine}} | deity = [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]] | Shrine rank = | founded = 1611 (Keichō 16) | main hall = [[Nagare-zukuri]] | Other name = Myōken Nakamura Shrine | festival = April 18–19 | rituals = [[Sōma Nomaoi]] (July 23–25) | map = {{location map|Japan Fukushima Prefecture|label=Sōma Nakamura Shrine|float=center|relief=1|width=200|caption=}} }} '''Sōma Nakamura Shrine''' (相馬中村神社) is a Shinto shrine located in Nakamura, Sōma City, Fukushima Prefecture. It is also known as '''Myōken Nakamura Shrine''' (妙見中村神社) or simply Nakamura Shrine. The enshrined deity is {{ill|Ame-no-Minakanushi|ja|天之御中主神}} (identified with {{ill|Myōken Bosatsu|ja|妙見菩薩}}). The shrine serves as the starting point for the {{ill|Sōma Nomaoi|ja|相馬野馬追}} festival.{{Cite web|url=https://www.minyu-net.com/news/news/FM20211115-667668.php |title=Sōma Nakamura Shrine to Receive New Grand Torii After Earthquake Damage |publisher=Fukushima Minyu Newspaper |accessdate=2021-11-15}} == Overview == The origins of Sōma Nakamura Shrine trace back to the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}} when {{ill|Taira no Masakado|ja|Taira no Masakado|1=Taira no Masakado}}, the founder of the {{ill|Sōma clan|ja|相馬氏}}, established a Myōken Shrine in {{ill|Shimōsa Province|ja|下総国}} during the Jōhei era (931–937). As the Sōma clan moved to the Sōma district, a new shrine was established. By the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}} in the late 16th century, {{ill|Nakamura Castle (Mutsu Province)|ja|中村城 (Mutsu Province )}} became the clan's northern stronghold, governed by lords such as {{ill|Sōma Moritane|ja|相馬盛胤}} and {{ill|Sōma Takamine|ja|相馬隆胤}}. After the {{ill|Battle of Sekigahara|ja|関ヶ原の戦い}} in 1600, the Sōma clan was briefly dispossessed of their holdings but regained control in 1611, establishing the {{ill|Sōma Nakamura Domain|ja|相馬中村藩}}. The shrine was built by {{ill|Sōma Takatane|ja|相馬利胤}}, the first lord of the Nakamura Domain, near the southwest of Nakamura Castle in 1611. The current main shrine building was constructed in {{ill|1643|ja|寛永20年}} by {{ill|Sōma Yoshitane|ja|相馬義胤 (相馬中村藩主)}}, the second domain lord, and has been designated as a {{ill|National Important Cultural Property|ja|重要文化財}}. During the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離}} led to the removal of Myōken Bosatsu, and the shrine was renamed Sōma Nakamura Shrine. Another shrine, {{ill|Sōma Shrine (Sōma City)|lt=Sōma Shrine|ja|相馬神社 (相馬市)}}, was established in 1880 on the ruins of Nakamura Castle's main keep after the {{ill|Boshin War|ja|戊辰戦争}}. The shrine has been under the care of the Tashiro family, who have served as hereditary priests since its establishment. The shrine formerly maintained stables for horses, including retired racehorses such as {{ill|Basikeen|ja|バシケーン}} (winner of the 2010 Nakayama Grand Jump) and {{ill|Million Disc|ja|ミリオンディスク}} (winner of the 2009 Capella Stakes). However, the stables were dismantled around 2019.[https://news.netkeiba.com/?pid=news_view&no=72447 Basikeen Finds a New Home at Sōma Nakamura Shrine] == Cultural Properties == '''Important Cultural Properties''' * Main Shrine, Worship Hall, and Offering Hall (1 building) ''' Includes a "kuden" (宮殿) altar. == images == File:Soma otemon.JPG|{{ill|Nakamura Castle (Mutsu Province)|ja|中村城 (Mutsu Province )}} Otemon Gate (2013) File:Nakamura shrine 003.JPG|Entrance to Nakamura Shrine, featuring a horse statue associated with {{ill|Sōma Nomaoi|ja|相馬野馬追}} (2013) == See Also == * {{ill|Nakamura {{infobox religious building | name = Sōma Nakamura Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Somanakamura shrine.jpg|270px]]
Haiden {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=270}} | location = 140 Nakamura, Kitamachi, Sōma City, Fukushima Prefecture, Japan | coordinates = {{coord|37|47|49.65|N|140|54|48.45|E|region:JP-07_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Sōma Nakamura Shrine}} | deity = [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]] | Shrine rank = | founded = 1611 (Keichō 16) | main hall = [[Nagare-zukuri]] | Other name = Myōken Nakamura Shrine | festival = April 18–19 | rituals = [[Sōma Nomaoi]] (July 23–25) | map = {{location map|Japan Fukushima Prefecture|label=Sōma Nakamura Shrine|float=center|relief=1|width=200|caption=}} }} '''Sōma Nakamura Shrine''' (相馬中村神社) is a Shinto shrine located in Nakamura, Sōma City, Fukushima Prefecture. It is also known as '''Myōken Nakamura Shrine''' (妙見中村神社) or simply Nakamura Shrine. The enshrined deity is {{ill|Ame-no-Minakanushi|ja|天之御中主神}} (identified with {{ill|Myōken Bosatsu|ja|妙見菩薩}}). The shrine serves as the starting point for the {{ill|Sōma Nomaoi|ja|相馬野馬追}} festival.{{Cite web|url=https://www.minyu-net.com/news/news/FM20211115-667668.php |title=Sōma Nakamura Shrine to Receive New Grand Torii After Earthquake Damage |publisher=Fukushima Minyu Newspaper |accessdate=2021-11-15}} == Overview == The origins of Sōma Nakamura Shrine trace back to the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}} when {{ill|Taira no Masakado|ja|Taira no Masakado|1=Taira no Masakado}}, the founder of the {{ill|Sōma clan|ja|相馬氏}}, established a Myōken Shrine in {{ill|Shimōsa Province|ja|下総国}} during the Jōhei era (931–937). As the Sōma clan moved to the Sōma district, a new shrine was established. By the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}} in the late 16th century, {{ill|Nakamura Castle (Mutsu Province)|ja|中村城 (Mutsu Province )}} became the clan's northern stronghold, governed by lords such as {{ill|Sōma Moritane|ja|相馬盛胤}} and {{ill|Sōma Takamine|ja|相馬隆胤}}. After the {{ill|Battle of Sekigahara|ja|関ヶ原の戦い}} in 1600, the Sōma clan was briefly dispossessed of their holdings but regained control in 1611, establishing the {{ill|Sōma Nakamura Domain|ja|相馬中村藩}}. The shrine was built by {{ill|Sōma Takatane|ja|相馬利胤}}, the first lord of the Nakamura Domain, near the southwest of Nakamura Castle in 1611. The current main shrine building was constructed in {{ill|1643|ja|寛永20年}} by {{ill|Sōma Yoshitane|ja|相馬義胤 (相馬中村藩主)}}, the second domain lord, and has been designated as a {{ill|National Important Cultural Property|ja|重要文化財}}. During the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離}} led to the removal of Myōken Bosatsu, and the shrine was renamed Sōma Nakamura Shrine. Another shrine, {{ill|Sōma Shrine (Sōma City)|lt=Sōma Shrine|ja|相馬神社 (相馬市)}}, was established in 1880 on the ruins of Nakamura Castle's main keep after the {{ill|Boshin War|ja|戊辰戦争}}. The shrine has been under the care of the Tashiro family, who have served as hereditary priests since its establishment. The shrine formerly maintained stables for horses, including retired racehorses such as {{ill|Basikeen|ja|バシケーン}} (winner of the 2010 Nakayama Grand Jump) and {{ill|Million Disc|ja|ミリオンディスク}} (winner of the 2009 Capella Stakes). However, the stables were dismantled around 2019.[https://news.netkeiba.com/?pid=news_view&no=72447 Basikeen Finds a New Home at Sōma Nakamura Shrine] == Cultural Properties == '''Important Cultural Properties''' * Main Shrine, Worship Hall, and Offering Hall (1 building) ''' Includes a "kuden" (宮殿) altar. == images == File:Soma otemon.JPG|{{ill|Nakamura Castle (Mutsu Province)|ja|中村城 (Mutsu Province )}} Otemon Gate (2013) File:Nakamura shrine 003.JPG|Entrance to Nakamura Shrine, featuring a horse statue associated with {{ill|Sōma Nomaoi|ja|相馬野馬追}} (2013) == See Also == * {{ill|Nakamura Castle (Mutsu Province)|ja|中村城 (Mutsu Province )}} * {{ill|Sōma Nomaoi|ja|相馬野馬追}} == External Links == * [https://soumanakamurajinja.com/ Sōma Nakamura Shrine Official Website] * [https://ameblo.jp/umatoayumu-soma/ Uma to Ayumu SOMA Blog] ==References== {{reflist}} {{神道 横}} [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Sōma Nakamura Shrine]][[Category:qq]] {{infobox religious building | name = Sōma Nakamura Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Somanakamura shrine.jpg|270px]]
Haiden {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=270}} | location = 140 Nakamura, Kitamachi, Sōma City, Fukushima Prefecture, Japan | coordinates = {{coord|37|47|49.65|N|140|54|48.45|E|region:JP-07_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Sōma Nakamura Shrine}} | deity = [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]] | Shrine rank = | founded = 1611 (Keichō 16) | main hall = [[Nagare-zukuri]] | Other name = Myōken Nakamura Shrine | festival = April 18–19 | rituals = [[Sōma Nomaoi]] (July 23–25) | map = {{location map|Japan Fukushima Prefecture|label=Sōma Nakamura Shrine|float=center|relief=1|width=200|caption=}} }} '''Sōma Nakamura Shrine''' (相馬中村神社) is a Shinto shrine located in Nakamura, Sōma City, Fukushima Prefecture. It is also known as '''Myōken Nakamura Shrine''' (妙見中村神社) or simply Nakamura Shrine. The enshrined deity is {{ill|Ame-no-Minakanushi|ja|天之御中主神}} (identified with {{ill|Myōken Bosatsu|ja|妙見菩薩}}). The shrine serves as the starting point for the {{ill|Sōma Nomaoi|ja|相馬野馬追}} festival.{{Cite web|url=https://www.minyu-net.com/news/news/FM20211115-667668.php |title=Sōma Nakamura Shrine to Receive New Grand Torii After Earthquake Damage |publisher=Fukushima Minyu Newspaper |accessdate=2021-11-15}} == Overview == The origins of Sōma Nakamura Shrine trace back to the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}} when {{ill|Taira no Masakado|ja|Taira no Masakado|1=Taira no Masakado}}, the founder of the {{ill|Sōma clan|ja|相馬氏}}, established a Myōken Shrine in {{ill|Shimōsa Province|ja|下総国}} during the Jōhei era (931–937). As the Sōma clan moved to the Sōma district, a new shrine was established. By the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}} in the late 16th century, {{ill|Nakamura Castle (Mutsu Province)|ja|中村城 (Mutsu Province )}} became the clan's northern stronghold, governed by lords such as {{ill|Sōma Moritane|ja|相馬盛胤}} and {{ill|Sōma Takamine|ja|相馬隆胤}}. The shrine was built by {{ill|Sōma Takatane|ja|相馬利胤}}, the first lord of the Nakamura Domain, near the southwest of Nakamura Castle in 1611. The current main shrine building was constructed in {{ill|1643|ja|寛永20年}} by {{ill|Sōma Yoshitane|ja|相馬義胤 (相馬中村藩主)}}, the second domain lord, and has been designated as a {{ill|National Important Cultural Property|ja|重要文化財}}. During the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離}} led to the removal of Myōken Bosatsu, and the shrine was renamed Sōma Nakamura Shrine. Another shrine, {{ill|Sōma Shrine (Sōma City)|lt=Sōma Shrine|ja|相馬神社 (相馬市)}}, was established in 1880 on the ruins of Nakamura Castle's main keep after the {{ill|Boshin War|ja|戊辰戦争}}. The shrine has been under the care of the Tashiro family, who have served as hereditary priests since its establishment. The shrine formerly maintained stables for horses, including retired racehorses such as {{ill|Basikeen|ja|バシケーン}} (winner of the 2010 Nakayama Grand Jump) and {{ill|Million Disc|ja|ミリオンディスク}} (winner of the 2009 Capella Stakes). However, the stables were dismantled around 2019.[https://news.netkeiba.com/?pid=news_view&no=72447 Basikeen Finds a New Home at Sōma Nakamura Shrine] == Cultural Properties == '''Important Cultural Properties''' * Main Shrine, Worship Hall, and Offering Hall (1 building) ''' Includes a "kuden" (宮殿) altar. == images == File:Soma otemon.JPG|{{ill|Nakamura Castle (Mutsu Province)|ja|中村城 (Mutsu Province )}} Otemon Gate (2013) File:Nakamura shrine 003.JPG|Entrance to Nakamura Shrine, featuring a horse statue associated with {{ill|Sōma Nomaoi|ja|相馬野馬追}} (2013) == See Also == * {{ill|Nakamura {{infobox religious building | name = Sōma Nakamura Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Somanakamura shrine.jpg|270px]]
Haiden {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=270}} | location = 140 Nakamura, Kitamachi, Sōma City, Fukushima Prefecture, Japan | coordinates = {{coord|37|47|49.65|N|140|54|48.45|E|region:JP-07_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Sōma Nakamura Shrine}} | deity = [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]] | Shrine rank = | founded = 1611 (Keichō 16) | main hall = [[Nagare-zukuri]] | Other name = Myōken Nakamura Shrine | festival = April 18–19 | rituals = [[Sōma Nomaoi]] (July 23–25) | map = {{location map|Japan Fukushima Prefecture|label=Sōma Nakamura Shrine|float=center|relief=1|width=200|caption=}} }} '''Sōma Nakamura Shrine''' (相馬中村神社) is a Shinto shrine located in Nakamura, Sōma City, Fukushima Prefecture. It is also known as '''Myōken Nakamura Shrine''' (妙見中村神社) or simply Nakamura Shrine. The enshrined deity is {{ill|Ame-no-Minakanushi|ja|天之御中主神}} (identified with {{ill|Myōken Bosatsu|ja|妙見菩薩}}). The shrine serves as the starting point for the {{ill|Sōma Nomaoi|ja|相馬野馬追}} festival.{{Cite web|url=https://www.minyu-net.com/news/news/FM20211115-667668.php |title=Sōma Nakamura Shrine to Receive New Grand Torii After Earthquake Damage |publisher=Fukushima Minyu Newspaper |accessdate=2021-11-15}} == Overview == The origins of Sōma Nakamura Shrine trace back to the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}} when {{ill|Taira no Masakado|ja|Taira no Masakado|1=Taira no Masakado}}, the founder of the {{ill|Sōma clan|ja|相馬氏}}, established a Myōken Shrine in {{ill|Shimōsa Province|ja|下総国}} during the Jōhei era (931–937). As the Sōma clan moved to the Sōma district, a new shrine was established. By the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}} in the late 16th century, {{ill|Nakamura Castle (Mutsu Province)|ja|中村城 (Mutsu Province )}} became the clan's northern stronghold, governed by lords such as {{ill|Sōma Moritane|ja|相馬盛胤}} and {{ill|Sōma Takamine|ja|相馬隆胤}}. After the {{ill|Battle of Sekigahara|ja|関ヶ原の戦い}} in 1600, the Sōma clan was briefly dispossessed of their holdings but regained control in 1611, establishing the {{ill|Sōma Nakamura Domain|ja|相馬中村藩}}. The shrine was built by {{ill|Sōma Takatane|ja|相馬利胤}}, the first lord of the Nakamura Domain, near the southwest of Nakamura Castle in 1611. The current main shrine building was constructed in {{ill|1643|ja|寛永20年}} by {{ill|Sōma Yoshitane|ja|相馬義胤 (相馬中村藩主)}}, the second domain lord, and has been designated as a {{ill|National Important Cultural Property|ja|重要文化財}}. During the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離}} led to the removal of Myōken Bosatsu, and the shrine was renamed Sōma Nakamura Shrine. Another shrine, {{ill|Sōma Shrine (Sōma City)|lt=Sōma Shrine|ja|相馬神社 (相馬市)}}, was established in 1880 on the ruins of Nakamura Castle's main keep after the {{ill|Boshin War|ja|戊辰戦争}}. The shrine has been under the care of the Tashiro family, who have served as hereditary priests since its establishment. The shrine formerly maintained stables for horses, including retired racehorses such as {{ill|Basikeen|ja|バシケーン}} (winner of the 2010 Nakayama Grand Jump) and {{ill|Million Disc|ja|ミリオンディスク}} (winner of the 2009 Capella Stakes). However, the stables were dismantled around 2019.[https://news.netkeiba.com/?pid=news_view&no=72447 Basikeen Finds a New Home at Sōma Nakamura Shrine] == Cultural Properties == '''Important Cultural Properties''' * Main Shrine, Worship Hall, and Offering Hall (1 building) ''' Includes a "kuden" (宮殿) altar. == images == File:Soma otemon.JPG|{{ill|Nakamura Castle (Mutsu Province)|ja|中村城 (Mutsu Province )}} Otemon Gate (2013) File:Nakamura shrine 003.JPG|Entrance to Nakamura Shrine, featuring a horse statue associated with {{ill|Sōma Nomaoi|ja|相馬野馬追}} (2013) == See Also == * {{ill|Nakamura Castle (Mutsu Province)|ja|中村城 (Mutsu Province )}} * {{ill|Sōma Nomaoi|ja|相馬野馬追}} == External Links == * [https://soumanakamurajinja.com/ Sōma Nakamura Shrine Official Website] * [https://ameblo.jp/umatoayumu-soma/ Uma to Ayumu SOMA Blog] ==References== {{reflist}} {{神道 横}} [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Sōma Nakamura Shrine]][[Category:qq]] revision.text:
{{infobox religious building | name = Sōma Nakamura Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Somanakamura shrine.jpg|270px]]
Haiden {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=270}} | location = 140 Nakamura, Kitamachi, Sōma City, Fukushima Prefecture, Japan | coordinates = {{coord|37|47|49.65|N|140|54|48.45|E|region:JP-07_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Sōma Nakamura Shrine}} | deity = [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]] | Shrine rank = | founded = 1611 (Keichō 16) | main hall = [[Nagare-zukuri]] | Other name = Myōken Nakamura Shrine | festival = April 18–19 | rituals = [[Sōma Nomaoi]] (July 23–25) | map = {{location map|Japan Fukushima Prefecture|label=Sōma Nakamura Shrine|float=center|relief=1|width=200|caption=}} }} '''Sōma Nakamura Shrine''' (相馬中村神社) is a Shinto shrine located in Nakamura, Sōma City, Fukushima Prefecture. It is also known as '''Myōken Nakamura Shrine''' (妙見中村神社) or simply Nakamura Shrine. The enshrined deity is {{ill|Ame-no-Minakanushi|ja|天之御中主神}} (identified with {{ill|Myōken Bosatsu|ja|妙見菩薩}}). The shrine serves as the starting point for the {{ill|Sōma Nomaoi|ja|相馬野馬追}} festival.{{Cite web|url=https://www.minyu-net.com/news/news/FM20211115-667668.php |title=Sōma Nakamura Shrine to Receive New Grand Torii After Earthquake Damage |publisher=Fukushima Minyu Newspaper |accessdate=2021-11-15}} == Overview == The origins of Sōma Nakamura Shrine trace back to the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}} when {{ill|Taira no Masakado|ja|Taira no Masakado|1=Taira no Masakado}}, the founder of the {{ill|Sōma clan|ja|相馬氏}}, established a Myōken Shrine in {{ill|Shimōsa Province|ja|下総国}} during the Jōhei era (931–937). As the Sōma clan moved to the Sōma district, a new shrine was established. By the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}} in the late 16th century, {{ill|Nakamura Castle (Mutsu Province)|ja|中村城 (Mutsu Province )}} became the clan's northern stronghold, governed by lords such as {{ill|Sōma Moritane|ja|相馬盛胤}} and {{ill|Sōma Takamine|ja|相馬隆胤}}. After the {{ill|Battle of Sekigahara|ja|関ヶ原の戦い}} in 1600, the Sōma clan was briefly dispossessed of their holdings but regained control in 1611, establishing the {{ill|Sōma Nakamura Domain|ja|相馬中村藩}}. The shrine was built by {{ill|Sōma Takatane|ja|相馬利胤}}, the first lord of the Nakamura Domain, near the southwest of Nakamura Castle in 1611. The current main shrine building was constructed in {{ill|1643|ja|寛永20年}} by {{ill|Sōma Yoshitane|ja|相馬義胤 (相馬中村藩主)}}, the second domain lord, and has been designated as a {{ill|National Important Cultural Property|ja|重要文化財}}. During the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離}} led to the removal of Myōken Bosatsu, and the shrine was renamed Sōma Nakamura Shrine. Another shrine, {{ill|Sōma Shrine (Sōma City)|lt=Sōma Shrine|ja|相馬神社 (相馬市)}}, was established in 1880 on the ruins of Nakamura Castle's main keep after the {{ill|Boshin War|ja|戊辰戦争}}. The shrine has been under the care of the Tashiro family, who have served as hereditary priests since its establishment. The shrine formerly maintained stables for horses, including retired racehorses such as {{ill|Basikeen|ja|バシケーン}} (winner of the 2010 Nakayama Grand Jump) and {{ill|Million Disc|ja|ミリオンディスク}} (winner of the 2009 Capella Stakes). However, the stables were dismantled around 2019.[https://news.netkeiba.com/?pid=news_view&no=72447 Basikeen Finds a New Home at Sōma Nakamura Shrine] == Cultural Properties == '''Important Cultural Properties''' * Main Shrine, Worship Hall, and Offering Hall (1 building) ''' Includes a "kuden" (宮殿) altar. == images == File:Soma otemon.JPG|{{ill|Nakamura Castle (Mutsu Province)|ja|中村城 (Mutsu Province )}} Otemon Gate (2013) File:Nakamura shrine 003.JPG|Entrance to Nakamura Shrine, featuring a horse statue associated with {{ill|Sōma Nomaoi|ja|相馬野馬追}} (2013) == See Also == * {{ill|Nakamura Castle (Mutsu Province)|ja|中村城 (Mutsu Province )}} * {{ill|Sōma Nomaoi|ja|相馬野馬追}} == External Links == * [https://soumanakamurajinja.com/ Sōma Nakamura Shrine Official Website] * [https://ameblo.jp/umatoayumu-soma/ Uma to Ayumu SOMA Blog] ==References== {{reflist}} {{神道 横}} [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Sōma Nakamura Shrine]][[Category:qq]] {{infobox religious building | name = Sōma Nakamura Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Somanakamura shrine.jpg|270px]]
Haiden {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=270}} | location = 140 Nakamura, Kitamachi, Sōma City, Fukushima Prefecture, Japan | coordinates = {{coord|37|47|49.65|N|140|54|48.45|E|region:JP-07_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Sōma Nakamura Shrine}} | deity = [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]] | Shrine rank = | founded = 1611 (Keichō 16) | main hall = [[Nagare-zukuri]] | Other name = Myōken Nakamura Shrine | festival = April 18–19 | rituals = [[Sōma Nomaoi]] (July 23–25) | map = {{location map|Japan Fukushima Prefecture|label=Sōma Nakamura Shrine|float=center|relief=1|width=200|caption=}} }} '''Sōma Nakamura Shrine''' (相馬中村神社) is a Shinto shrine located in Nakamura, Sōma City, Fukushima Prefecture. It is also known as '''Myōken Nakamura Shrine''' (妙見中村神社) or simply Nakamura Shrine. The enshrined deity is {{ill|Ame-no-Minakanushi|ja|天之御中主神}} (identified with {{ill|Myōken Bosatsu|ja|妙見菩薩}}). The shrine serves as the starting point for the {{ill|Sōma Nomaoi|ja|相馬野馬追}} festival.{{Cite web|url=https://www.minyu-net.com/news/news/FM20211115-667668.php |title=Sōma Nakamura Shrine to Receive New Grand Torii After Earthquake Damage |publisher=Fukushima Minyu Newspaper |accessdate=2021-11-15}} == Overview == The origins of Sōma Nakamura Shrine trace back to the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}} when {{ill|Taira no Masakado|ja|Taira no Masakado|1=Taira no Masakado}}, the founder of the {{ill|Sōma clan|ja|相馬氏}}, established a Myōken Shrine in {{ill|Shimōsa Province|ja|下総国}} during the Jōhei era (931–937). As the Sōma clan moved to the Sōma district, a new shrine was established. By the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}} in the late 16th century, {{ill|Nakamura Castle (Mutsu Province)|ja|中村城 (Mutsu Province )}} became the clan's northern stronghold, governed by lords such as {{ill|Sōma Moritane|ja|相馬盛胤}} and {{ill|Sōma Takamine|ja|相馬隆胤}}. After the {{ill|Battle of Sekigahara|ja|関ヶ原の戦い}} in 1600, the Sōma clan was briefly dispossessed of their holdings but regained control in 1611, establishing the {{ill|Sōma Nakamura Domain|ja|相馬中村藩}}. The shrine was built by {{ill|Sōma Takatane|ja|相馬利胤}}, the first lord of the Nakamura Domain, near the southwest of Nakamura Castle in 1611. The current main shrine building was constructed in {{ill|1643|ja|寛永20年}} by {{ill|Sōma Yoshitane|ja|相馬義胤 (相馬中村藩主)}}, the second domain lord, and has been designated as a {{ill|National Important Cultural Property|ja|重要文化財}}. During the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離}} led to the removal of Myōken Bosatsu, and the shrine was renamed Sōma Nakamura Shrine. Another shrine, {{ill|Sōma Shrine (Sōma City)|lt=Sōma Shrine|ja|相馬神社 (相馬市)}}, was established in 1880 on the ruins of Nakamura Castle's main keep after the {{ill|Boshin War|ja|戊辰戦争}}. The shrine has been under the care of the Tashiro family, who have served as hereditary priests since its establishment. The shrine formerly maintained stables for horses, including retired racehorses such as {{ill|Basikeen|ja|バシケーン}} (winner of the 2010 Nakayama Grand Jump) and {{ill|Million Disc|ja|ミリオンディスク}} (winner of the 2009 Capella Stakes). However, the stables were dismantled around 2019.[https://news.netkeiba.com/?pid=news_view&no=72447 Basikeen Finds a New Home at Sōma Nakamura Shrine] == Cultural Properties == '''Important Cultural {{infobox religious building | name = Sōma Nakamura Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Somanakamura shrine.jpg|270px]]
Haiden {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=270}} | location = 140 Nakamura, Kitamachi, Sōma City, Fukushima Prefecture, Japan | coordinates = {{coord|37|47|49.65|N|140|54|48.45|E|region:JP-07_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Sōma Nakamura Shrine}} | deity = [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]] | Shrine rank = | founded = 1611 (Keichō 16) | main hall = [[Nagare-zukuri]] | Other name = Myōken Nakamura Shrine | festival = April 18–19 | rituals = [[Sōma Nomaoi]] (July 23–25) | map = {{location map|Japan Fukushima Prefecture|label=Sōma Nakamura Shrine|float=center|relief=1|width=200|caption=}} }} '''Sōma Nakamura Shrine''' (相馬中村神社) is a Shinto shrine located in Nakamura, Sōma City, Fukushima Prefecture. It is also known as '''Myōken Nakamura Shrine''' (妙見中村神社) or simply Nakamura Shrine. The enshrined deity is {{ill|Ame-no-Minakanushi|ja|天之御中主神}} (identified with {{ill|Myōken Bosatsu|ja|妙見菩薩}}). The shrine serves as the starting point for the {{ill|Sōma Nomaoi|ja|相馬野馬追}} festival.{{Cite web|url=https://www.minyu-net.com/news/news/FM20211115-667668.php |title=Sōma Nakamura Shrine to Receive New Grand Torii After Earthquake Damage |publisher=Fukushima Minyu Newspaper |accessdate=2021-11-15}} == Overview == The origins of Sōma Nakamura Shrine trace back to the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}} when {{ill|Taira no Masakado|ja|Taira no Masakado|1=Taira no Masakado}}, the founder of the {{ill|Sōma clan|ja|相馬氏}}, established a Myōken Shrine in {{ill|Shimōsa Province|ja|下総国}} during the Jōhei era (931–937). As the Sōma clan moved to the Sōma district, a new shrine was established. By the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}} in the late 16th century, {{ill|Nakamura Castle (Mutsu Province)|ja|中村城 (Mutsu Province )}} became the clan's northern stronghold, governed by lords such as {{ill|Sōma Moritane|ja|相馬盛胤}} and {{ill|Sōma Takamine|ja|相馬隆胤}}. After the {{ill|Battle of Sekigahara|ja|関ヶ原の戦い}} in 1600, the Sōma clan was briefly dispossessed of their holdings but regained control in 1611, establishing the {{ill|Sōma Nakamura Domain|ja|相馬中村藩}}. The shrine was built by {{ill|Sōma Takatane|ja|相馬利胤}}, the first lord of the Nakamura Domain, near the southwest of Nakamura Castle in 1611. The current main shrine building was constructed in {{ill|1643|ja|寛永20年}} by {{ill|Sōma Yoshitane|ja|相馬義胤 (相馬中村藩主)}}, the second domain lord, and has been designated as a {{ill|National Important Cultural Property|ja|重要文化財}}. During the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離}} led to the removal of Myōken Bosatsu, and the shrine was renamed Sōma Nakamura Shrine. Another shrine, {{ill|Sōma Shrine (Sōma City)|lt=Sōma Shrine|ja|相馬神社 (相馬市)}}, was established in 1880 on the ruins of Nakamura Castle's main keep after the {{ill|Boshin War|ja|戊辰戦争}}. The shrine has been under the care of the Tashiro family, who have served as hereditary priests since its establishment. The shrine formerly maintained stables for horses, including retired racehorses such as {{ill|Basikeen|ja|バシケーン}} (winner of the 2010 Nakayama Grand Jump) and {{ill|Million Disc|ja|ミリオンディスク}} (winner of the 2009 Capella Stakes). However, the stables were dismantled around 2019.[https://news.netkeiba.com/?pid=news_view&no=72447 Basikeen Finds a New Home at Sōma Nakamura Shrine] == Cultural Properties == '''Important Cultural Properties''' * Main Shrine, Worship Hall, and Offering Hall (1 building) ''' Includes a "kuden" (宮殿) altar. == images == File:Soma otemon.JPG|{{ill|Nakamura Castle (Mutsu Province)|ja|中村城 (Mutsu Province )}} Otemon Gate (2013) File:Nakamura shrine 003.JPG|Entrance to Nakamura Shrine, featuring a horse statue associated with {{ill|Sōma Nomaoi|ja|相馬野馬追}} (2013) == See Also == * {{ill|Nakamura Castle (Mutsu Province)|ja|中村城 (Mutsu Province )}} * {{ill|Sōma Nomaoi|ja|相馬野馬追}} == External Links == * [https://soumanakamurajinja.com/ Sōma Nakamura Shrine Official Website] * revision.comment: Text replacement - "↵↵== See Also ==" to "== See Also =="
revision.text:
{{infobox religious building | name = Sōma Nakamura Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Somanakamura shrine.jpg|270px]]
Haiden {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=270}} | location = 140 Nakamura, Kitamachi, Sōma City, Fukushima Prefecture, Japan | coordinates = {{coord|37|47|49.65|N|140|54|48.45|E|region:JP-07_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Sōma Nakamura Shrine}} | deity = [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]] | Shrine rank = | founded = 1611 (Keichō 16) | main hall = [[Nagare-zukuri]] | Other name = Myōken Nakamura Shrine | festival = April 18–19 | rituals = [[Sōma Nomaoi]] (July 23–25) | map = {{location map|Japan Fukushima Prefecture|label=Sōma Nakamura Shrine|float=center|relief=1|width=200|caption=}} }} '''Sōma Nakamura Shrine''' (相馬中村神社) is a Shinto shrine located in Nakamura, Sōma City, Fukushima Prefecture. It is also known as '''Myōken Nakamura Shrine''' (妙見中村神社) or simply Nakamura Shrine. The enshrined deity is {{ill|Ame-no-Minakanushi|ja|天之御中主神}} (identified with {{ill|Myōken Bosatsu|ja|妙見菩薩}}). The shrine serves as the starting point for the {{ill|Sōma Nomaoi|ja|相馬野馬追}} festival.{{Cite web|url=https://www.minyu-net.com/news/news/FM20211115-667668.php |title=Sōma Nakamura Shrine to Receive New Grand Torii After Earthquake Damage |publisher=Fukushima Minyu Newspaper |accessdate=2021-11-15}} == Overview == The origins of Sōma Nakamura Shrine trace back to the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}} when {{ill|Taira no Masakado|ja|Taira no Masakado|1=Taira no Masakado}}, the founder of the {{ill|Sōma clan|ja|相馬氏}}, established a Myōken Shrine in {{ill|Shimōsa Province|ja|下総国}} during the Jōhei era (931–937). As the Sōma clan moved to the Sōma district, a new shrine was established. By the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}} in the late 16th century, {{ill|Nakamura Castle (Mutsu Province)|ja|中村城 (Mutsu Province )}} became the clan's northern stronghold, governed by lords such as {{ill|Sōma Moritane|ja|相馬盛胤}} and {{ill|Sōma Takamine|ja|相馬隆胤}}. After the {{ill|Battle of Sekigahara|ja|関ヶ原の戦い}} in 1600, the Sōma clan was briefly dispossessed of their holdings but regained control in 1611, establishing the {{ill|Sōma Nakamura Domain|ja|相馬中村藩}}. The shrine was built by {{ill|Sōma Takatane|ja|相馬利胤}}, the first lord of the Nakamura Domain, near the southwest of Nakamura Castle in 1611. The current main shrine building was constructed in {{ill|1643|ja|寛永20年}} by {{ill|Sōma Yoshitane|ja|相馬義胤 (相馬中村藩主)}}, the second domain lord, and has been designated as a {{ill|National Important Cultural Property|ja|重要文化財}}. During the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離}} led to the removal of Myōken Bosatsu, and the shrine was renamed Sōma Nakamura Shrine. Another shrine, {{ill|Sōma Shrine (Sōma City)|lt=Sōma Shrine|ja|相馬神社 (相馬市)}}, was established in 1880 on the ruins of Nakamura Castle's main keep after the {{ill|Boshin War|ja|戊辰戦争}}. The shrine has been under the care of the Tashiro family, who have served as hereditary priests since its establishment. The shrine formerly maintained stables for horses, including retired racehorses such as {{ill|Basikeen|ja|バシケーン}} (winner of the 2010 Nakayama Grand Jump) and {{ill|Million Disc|ja|ミリオンディスク}} (winner of the 2009 Capella Stakes). However, the stables were dismantled around 2019.[https://news.netkeiba.com/?pid=news_view&no=72447 Basikeen Finds a New Home at Sōma Nakamura Shrine] == Cultural Properties == '''Important Cultural Properties''' * Main Shrine, Worship Hall, and Offering Hall (1 building) ''' Includes a "kuden" (宮殿) altar. == images == File:Soma otemon.JPG|{{ill|Nakamura Castle (Mutsu Province)|ja|中村城 (Mutsu Province )}} Otemon Gate (2013) File:Nakamura shrine 003.JPG|Entrance to Nakamura Shrine, featuring a horse statue associated with {{ill|Sōma Nomaoi|ja|相馬野馬追}} (2013) == See Also == * {{ill|Nakamura Castle (Mutsu Province)|ja|中村城 (Mutsu Province )}} * {{ill|Sōma Nomaoi|ja|相馬野馬追}} == External Links == * [https://soumanakamurajinja.com/ Sōma Nakamura Shrine Official Website] * [https://ameblo.jp/umatoayumu-soma/ Uma to Ayumu SOMA Blog] ==References== {{reflist}} {{神道 横}} [[Category:Completion tier 2]][[Category: tier 2]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Sōma Nakamura {{infobox religious building | name = Sōma Nakamura Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Somanakamura shrine.jpg|270px]]
Haiden {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=270}} | location = 140 Nakamura, Kitamachi, Sōma City, Fukushima Prefecture, Japan | coordinates = {{coord|37|47|49.65|N|140|54|48.45|E|region:JP-07_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Sōma Nakamura Shrine}} | deity = [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]] | Shrine rank = | founded = 1611 (Keichō 16) | main hall = [[Nagare-zukuri]] | Other name = Myōken Nakamura Shrine | festival = April 18–19 | rituals = [[Sōma Nomaoi]] (July 23–25) | map = {{location map|Japan Fukushima Prefecture|label=Sōma Nakamura Shrine|float=center|relief=1|width=200|caption=}} }} '''Sōma Nakamura Shrine''' (相馬中村神社) is a Shinto shrine located in Nakamura, Sōma City, Fukushima Prefecture. It is also known as '''Myōken Nakamura Shrine''' (妙見中村神社) or simply Nakamura Shrine. The enshrined deity is {{ill|Ame-no-Minakanushi|ja|天之御中主神}} (identified with {{ill|Myōken Bosatsu|ja|妙見菩薩}}). The shrine serves as the starting point for the {{ill|Sōma Nomaoi|ja|相馬野馬追}} festival.{{Cite web|url=https://www.minyu-net.com/news/news/FM20211115-667668.php |title=Sōma Nakamura Shrine to Receive New Grand Torii After Earthquake Damage |publisher=Fukushima Minyu Newspaper |accessdate=2021-11-15}} == Overview == The origins of Sōma Nakamura Shrine trace back to the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}} when {{ill|Taira no Masakado|ja|Taira no Masakado|1=Taira no Masakado}}, the founder of the {{ill|Sōma clan|ja|相馬氏}}, established a Myōken Shrine in {{ill|Shimōsa Province|ja|下総国}} during the Jōhei era (931–937). As the Sōma clan moved to the Sōma district, a new shrine was established. By the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}} in the late 16th century, {{ill|Nakamura Castle (Mutsu Province)|ja|中村城 (Mutsu Province )}} became the clan's northern stronghold, governed by lords such as {{ill|Sōma Moritane|ja|相馬盛胤}} and {{ill|Sōma Takamine|ja|相馬隆胤}}. After the {{ill|Battle of Sekigahara|ja|関ヶ原の戦い}} in 1600, the Sōma clan was briefly dispossessed of their holdings but regained control in 1611, establishing the {{ill|Sōma Nakamura Domain|ja|相馬中村藩}}. The shrine was built by {{ill|Sōma Takatane|ja|相馬利胤}}, the first lord of the Nakamura Domain, near the southwest of Nakamura Castle in 1611. The current main shrine building was constructed in {{ill|1643|ja|寛永20年}} by {{ill|Sōma Yoshitane|ja|相馬義胤 (相馬中村藩主)}}, the second domain lord, and has been designated as a {{ill|National Important Cultural Property|ja|重要文化財}}. During the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離}} led to the removal of Myōken Bosatsu, and the shrine was renamed Sōma Nakamura Shrine. Another shrine, {{ill|Sōma Shrine (Sōma City)|lt=Sōma Shrine|ja|相馬神社 (相馬市)}}, was established in 1880 on the ruins of Nakamura Castle's main keep after the {{ill|Boshin War|ja|戊辰戦争}}. The shrine has been under the care of the Tashiro family, who have served as hereditary priests since its establishment. The shrine formerly maintained stables for horses, including retired racehorses such as {{ill|Basikeen|ja|バシケーン}} (winner of the 2010 Nakayama Grand Jump) and {{ill|Million Disc|ja|ミリオンディスク}} (winner of the 2009 Capella Stakes). However, the stables were dismantled around 2019.[https://news.netkeiba.com/?pid=news_view&no=72447 Basikeen Finds a New Home at Sōma Nakamura Shrine] == Cultural Properties == '''Important Cultural revision.text:
{{infobox religious building | name = Sōma Nakamura Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Somanakamura shrine.jpg|270px]]
Haiden {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=270}} | location = 140 Nakamura, Kitamachi, Sōma City, Fukushima Prefecture, Japan | coordinates = {{coord|37|47|49.65|N|140|54|48.45|E|region:JP-07_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Sōma Nakamura Shrine}} | deity = [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]] | Shrine rank = | founded = 1611 (Keichō 16) | main hall = [[Nagare-zukuri]] | Other name = Myōken Nakamura Shrine | festival = April 18–19 | rituals = [[Sōma Nomaoi]] (July 23–25) | map = {{location map|Japan Fukushima Prefecture|label=Sōma Nakamura Shrine|float=center|relief=1|width=200|caption=}} }} '''Sōma Nakamura Shrine''' (相馬中村神社) is a Shinto shrine located in Nakamura, Sōma City, Fukushima Prefecture. It is also known as '''Myōken Nakamura Shrine''' (妙見中村神社) or simply Nakamura Shrine. The enshrined deity is {{ill|Ame-no-Minakanushi|ja|天之御中主神}} (identified with {{ill|Myōken Bosatsu|ja|妙見菩薩}}). The shrine serves as the starting point for the {{ill|Sōma Nomaoi|ja|相馬野馬追}} festival.{{Cite web|url=https://www.minyu-net.com/news/news/FM20211115-667668.php |title=Sōma Nakamura Shrine to Receive New Grand Torii After Earthquake Damage |publisher=Fukushima Minyu Newspaper |accessdate=2021-11-15}} == Overview == The origins of Sōma Nakamura Shrine trace back to the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}} when {{ill|Taira no Masakado|ja|Taira no Masakado|1=Taira no Masakado}}, the founder of the {{ill|Sōma clan|ja|相馬氏}}, established a Myōken Shrine in {{ill|Shimōsa Province|ja|下総国}} during the Jōhei era (931–937). As the Sōma clan moved to the Sōma district, a new shrine was established. By the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}} in the late 16th century, {{ill|Nakamura Castle (Mutsu Province)|ja|中村城 (Mutsu Province )}} became the clan's northern stronghold, governed by lords such as {{ill|Sōma Moritane|ja|相馬盛胤}} and {{ill|Sōma Takamine|ja|相馬隆胤}}. After the {{ill|Battle of Sekigahara|ja|関ヶ原の戦い}} in 1600, the Sōma clan was briefly dispossessed of their holdings but regained control in 1611, establishing the {{ill|Sōma Nakamura Domain|ja|相馬中村藩}}. The shrine was built by {{ill|Sōma Takatane|ja|相馬利胤}}, the first lord of the Nakamura Domain, near the southwest of Nakamura Castle in 1611. The current main shrine building was constructed in {{ill|1643|ja|寛永20年}} by {{ill|Sōma Yoshitane|ja|相馬義胤 (相馬中村藩主)}}, the second domain lord, and has been designated as a {{ill|National Important Cultural Property|ja|重要文化財}}. During the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離}} led to the removal of Myōken Bosatsu, and the shrine was renamed Sōma Nakamura Shrine. Another shrine, {{ill|Sōma Shrine (Sōma City)|lt=Sōma Shrine|ja|相馬神社 (相馬市)}}, was established in 1880 on the ruins of Nakamura Castle's main keep after the {{ill|Boshin War|ja|戊辰戦争}}. The shrine has been under the care of the Tashiro family, who have served as hereditary priests since its establishment. The shrine formerly maintained stables for horses, including retired racehorses such as {{ill|Basikeen|ja|バシケーン}} (winner of the 2010 Nakayama Grand Jump) and {{ill|Million Disc|ja|ミリオンディスク}} (winner of the 2009 Capella Stakes). However, the stables were dismantled around 2019.[https://news.netkeiba.com/?pid=news_view&no=72447 Basikeen Finds a New Home at Sōma Nakamura Shrine] == Cultural Properties == '''Important Cultural Properties''' * Main Shrine, Worship Hall, and Offering Hall (1 building) ''' Includes a "kuden" (宮殿) altar. == images == File:Soma otemon.JPG|{{ill|Nakamura Castle (Mutsu Province)|ja|中村城 (Mutsu Province )}} Otemon Gate (2013) File:Nakamura shrine 003.JPG|Entrance to Nakamura Shrine, featuring a horse statue associated with {{ill|Sōma Nomaoi|ja|相馬野馬追}} (2013) == See Also == * {{ill|Nakamura Castle (Mutsu Province)|ja|中村城 (Mutsu Province )}} * {{ill|Sōma Nomaoi|ja|相馬野馬追}} == External Links == * [https://soumanakamurajinja.com/ Sōma Nakamura Shrine Official Website] * revision.comment: Text replacement - "== See Also ==" to " == See Also! =="
revision.text:
{{infobox religious building | name = Sōma Nakamura Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Somanakamura shrine.jpg|270px]]
Haiden {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=270}} | location = 140 Nakamura, Kitamachi, Sōma City, Fukushima Prefecture, Japan | coordinates = {{coord|37|47|49.65|N|140|54|48.45|E|region:JP-07_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Sōma Nakamura Shrine}} | deity = [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]] | Shrine rank = | founded = 1611 (Keichō 16) | main hall = [[Nagare-zukuri]] | Other name = Myōken Nakamura Shrine | festival = April 18–19 | rituals = [[Sōma Nomaoi]] (July 23–25) | map = {{location map|Japan Fukushima Prefecture|label=Sōma Nakamura Shrine|float=center|relief=1|width=200|caption=}} }} '''Sōma Nakamura Shrine''' (相馬中村神社) is a Shinto shrine located in Nakamura, Sōma City, Fukushima Prefecture. It is also known as '''Myōken Nakamura Shrine''' (妙見中村神社) or simply Nakamura Shrine. The enshrined deity is {{ill|Ame-no-Minakanushi|ja|天之御中主神}} (identified with {{ill|Myōken Bosatsu|ja|妙見菩薩}}). The shrine serves as the starting point for the {{ill|Sōma Nomaoi|ja|相馬野馬追}} festival.{{Cite web|url=https://www.minyu-net.com/news/news/FM20211115-667668.php |title=Sōma Nakamura Shrine to Receive New Grand Torii After Earthquake Damage |publisher=Fukushima Minyu Newspaper |accessdate=2021-11-15}} == Overview == The origins of Sōma Nakamura Shrine trace back to the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}} when {{ill|Taira no Masakado|ja|Taira no Masakado|1=Taira no Masakado}}, the founder of the {{ill|Sōma clan|ja|相馬氏}}, established a Myōken Shrine in {{ill|Shimōsa Province|ja|下総国}} during the Jōhei era (931–937). As the Sōma clan moved to the Sōma district, a new shrine was established. By the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}} in the late 16th century, {{ill|Nakamura Castle (Mutsu Province)|ja|中村城 (Mutsu Province )}} became the clan's northern stronghold, governed by lords such as {{ill|Sōma Moritane|ja|相馬盛胤}} and {{ill|Sōma Takamine|ja|相馬隆胤}}. After the {{ill|Battle of Sekigahara|ja|関ヶ原の戦い}} in 1600, the Sōma clan was briefly dispossessed of their holdings but regained control in 1611, establishing the {{ill|Sōma Nakamura Domain|ja|相馬中村藩}}. The shrine was built by {{ill|Sōma Takatane|ja|相馬利胤}}, the first lord of the Nakamura Domain, near the southwest of Nakamura Castle in 1611. The current main shrine building was constructed in {{ill|1643|ja|寛永20年}} by {{ill|Sōma Yoshitane|ja|相馬義胤 (相馬中村藩主)}}, the second domain lord, and has been designated as a {{ill|National Important Cultural Property|ja|重要文化財}}. During the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離}} led to the removal of Myōken Bosatsu, and the shrine was renamed Sōma Nakamura Shrine. Another shrine, {{ill|Sōma Shrine (Sōma City)|lt=Sōma Shrine|ja|相馬神社 (相馬市)}}, was established in 1880 on the ruins of Nakamura Castle's main keep after the {{ill|Boshin War|ja|戊辰戦争}}. The shrine has been under the care of the Tashiro family, who have served as hereditary priests since its establishment. The shrine formerly maintained stables for horses, including retired racehorses such as {{ill|Basikeen|ja|バシケーン}} (winner of the 2010 Nakayama Grand Jump) and {{ill|Million Disc|ja|ミリオンディスク}} (winner of the 2009 Capella Stakes). However, the stables were dismantled around 2019.[https://news.netkeiba.com/?pid=news_view&no=72447 Basikeen Finds a New Home at Sōma Nakamura Shrine] == Cultural Properties == '''Important Cultural Properties''' * Main Shrine, Worship Hall, and Offering Hall (1 building) ''' Includes a "kuden" (宮殿) altar. == images == File:Soma otemon.JPG|{{ill|Nakamura Castle (Mutsu Province)|ja|中村城 (Mutsu Province )}} Otemon Gate (2013) File:Nakamura shrine 003.JPG|Entrance to Nakamura Shrine, featuring a horse statue associated with {{ill|Sōma Nomaoi|ja|相馬野馬追}} (2013) == See Also! == * {{ill|Nakamura Castle (Mutsu Province)|ja|中村城 (Mutsu Province )}} * {{ill|Sōma Nomaoi|ja|相馬野馬追}} == External Links == * [https://soumanakamurajinja.com/ Sōma Nakamura Shrine Official Website] * [https://ameblo.jp/umatoayumu-soma/ Uma to Ayumu SOMA Blog] ==References== {{reflist}} {{神道 横}} [[Category:Completion tier 2]][[Category: tier 2]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Sōma Nakamura
