{{infobox religious building | name = Sōma Nakamura Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Somanakamura shrine.jpg|270px]]
Haiden {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=270}} | location = 140 Nakamura, Kitamachi, Sōma City, Fukushima Prefecture, Japan | coordinates = {{coord|37|47|49.65|N|140|54|48.45|E|region:JP-07_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Sōma Nakamura Shrine}} | deity = [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]] | Shrine rank = | founded = 1611 (Keichō 16) | main hall = [[Nagare-zukuri]] | Other name = Myōken Nakamura Shrine | festival = April 18–19 | rituals = [[Sōma Nomaoi]] (July 23–25) | map = {{location map|Japan Fukushima Prefecture|label=Sōma Nakamura Shrine|float=center|relief=1|width=200|caption=}} }} '''Sōma Nakamura Shrine''' (相馬中村神社) is a Shinto shrine located in Nakamura, Sōma City, Fukushima Prefecture. It is also known as '''Myōken Nakamura Shrine''' (妙見中村神社) or simply Nakamura Shrine. The enshrined deity is {{ill|Ame-no-Minakanushi|ja|天之御中主神}} (identified with {{ill|Myōken Bosatsu|ja|妙見菩薩}}). The shrine serves as the starting point for the {{ill|Sōma Nomaoi|ja|相馬野馬追}} festival.{{Cite web|url=https://www.minyu-net.com/news/news/FM20211115-667668.php |title=Sōma Nakamura Shrine to Receive New Grand Torii After Earthquake Damage |publisher=Fukushima Minyu Newspaper |accessdate=2021-11-15}} == Overview == The origins of Sōma Nakamura Shrine trace back to the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}} when {{ill|Taira no Masakado|ja|Taira no Masakado|1=Taira no Masakado}}, the founder of the {{ill|Sōma clan|ja|相馬氏}}, established a Myōken Shrine in {{ill|Shimōsa Province|ja|下総国}} during the Jōhei era (931–937). As the Sōma clan moved to the Sōma district, a new shrine was established. By the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}} in the late 16th century, {{ill|Nakamura Castle (Mutsu Province)|ja|中村城 (Mutsu Province )}} became the clan's northern stronghold, governed by lords such as {{ill|Sōma Moritane|ja|相馬盛胤}} and {{ill|Sōma Takamine|ja|相馬隆胤}}. After the {{ill|Battle of Sekigahara|ja|関ヶ原の戦い}} in 1600, the Sōma clan was briefly dispossessed of their holdings but regained control in 1611, establishing the {{ill|Sōma Nakamura Domain|ja|相馬中村藩}}. The shrine was built by {{ill|Sōma Takatane|ja|相馬利胤}}, the first lord of the Nakamura Domain, near the southwest of Nakamura Castle in 1611. The current main shrine building was constructed in {{ill|1643|ja|寛永20年}} by {{ill|Sōma Yoshitane|ja|相馬義胤 (相馬中村藩主)}}, the second domain lord, and has been designated as a {{ill|National Important Cultural Property|ja|重要文化財}}. During the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離}} led to the removal of Myōken Bosatsu, and the shrine was renamed Sōma Nakamura Shrine. Another shrine, {{ill|Sōma Shrine (Sōma City)|lt=Sōma Shrine|ja|相馬神社 (相馬市)}}, was established in 1880 on the ruins of Nakamura Castle's main keep after the {{ill|Boshin War|ja|戊辰戦争}}. The shrine has been under the care of the Tashiro family, who have served as hereditary priests since its establishment. The shrine formerly maintained stables for horses, including retired racehorses such as {{ill|Basikeen|ja|バシケーン}} (winner of the 2010 Nakayama Grand Jump) and {{ill|Million Disc|ja|ミリオンディスク}} (winner of the 2009 Capella Stakes). However, the stables were dismantled around 2019.[https://news.netkeiba.com/?pid=news_view&no=72447 Basikeen Finds a New Home at Sōma Nakamura Shrine] == Cultural Properties == '''Important Cultural Properties''' * Main Shrine, Worship Hall, and Offering Hall (1 building) ''' Includes a "kuden" (宮殿) altar. == images == File:Soma otemon.JPG|{{ill|Nakamura Castle (Mutsu Province)|ja|中村城 (Mutsu Province )}} Otemon Gate (2013) File:Nakamura shrine 003.JPG|Entrance to Nakamura Shrine, featuring a horse statue associated with {{ill|Sōma Nomaoi|ja|相馬野馬追}} (2013) == See Also! == * {{ill|Nakamura Castle (Mutsu Province)|ja|中村城 (Mutsu Province )}} * {{ill|Sōma Nomaoi|ja|相馬野馬追}} == External Links == * [https://soumanakamurajinja.com/ Sōma Nakamura Shrine Official Website] * revision.text:
{{infobox religious building | name = Sōma Nakamura Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Somanakamura shrine.jpg|270px]]
Haiden {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=270}} | location = 140 Nakamura, Kitamachi, Sōma City, Fukushima Prefecture, Japan | coordinates = {{coord|37|47|49.65|N|140|54|48.45|E|region:JP-07_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Sōma Nakamura Shrine}} | deity = [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]] | Shrine rank = | founded = 1611 (Keichō 16) | main hall = [[Nagare-zukuri]] | Other name = Myōken Nakamura Shrine | festival = April 18–19 | rituals = [[Sōma Nomaoi]] (July 23–25) | map = {{location map|Japan Fukushima Prefecture|label=Sōma Nakamura Shrine|float=center|relief=1|width=200|caption=}} }} '''Sōma Nakamura Shrine''' (相馬中村神社) is a Shinto shrine located in Nakamura, Sōma City, Fukushima Prefecture. It is also known as '''Myōken Nakamura Shrine''' (妙見中村神社) or simply Nakamura Shrine. The enshrined deity is {{ill|Ame-no-Minakanushi|ja|天之御中主神}} (identified with {{ill|Myōken Bosatsu|ja|妙見菩薩}}). The shrine serves as the starting point for the {{ill|Sōma Nomaoi|ja|相馬野馬追}} festival.{{Cite web|url=https://www.minyu-net.com/news/news/FM20211115-667668.php |title=Sōma Nakamura Shrine to Receive New Grand Torii After Earthquake Damage |publisher=Fukushima Minyu Newspaper |accessdate=2021-11-15}} == Overview == The origins of Sōma Nakamura Shrine trace back to the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}} when {{ill|Taira no Masakado|ja|Taira no Masakado|1=Taira no Masakado}}, the founder of the {{ill|Sōma clan|ja|相馬氏}}, established a Myōken Shrine in {{ill|Shimōsa Province|ja|下総国}} during the Jōhei era (931–937). As the Sōma clan moved to the Sōma district, a new shrine was established. By the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}} in the late 16th century, {{ill|Nakamura Castle (Mutsu Province)|ja|中村城 (Mutsu Province )}} became the clan's northern stronghold, governed by lords such as {{ill|Sōma Moritane|ja|相馬盛胤}} and {{ill|Sōma Takamine|ja|相馬隆胤}}. After the {{ill|Battle of Sekigahara|ja|関ヶ原の戦い}} in 1600, the Sōma clan was briefly dispossessed of their holdings but regained control in 1611, establishing the {{ill|Sōma Nakamura Domain|ja|相馬中村藩}}. The shrine was built by {{ill|Sōma Takatane|ja|相馬利胤}}, the first lord of the Nakamura Domain, near the southwest of Nakamura Castle in 1611. The current main shrine building was constructed in {{ill|1643|ja|寛永20年}} by {{ill|Sōma Yoshitane|ja|相馬義胤 (相馬中村藩主)}}, the second domain lord, and has been designated as a {{ill|National Important Cultural Property|ja|重要文化財}}. During the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離}} led to the removal of Myōken Bosatsu, and the shrine was renamed Sōma Nakamura Shrine. Another shrine, {{ill|Sōma Shrine (Sōma City)|lt=Sōma Shrine|ja|相馬神社 (相馬市)}}, was established in 1880 on the ruins of Nakamura Castle's main keep after the {{ill|Boshin War|ja|戊辰戦争}}. The shrine has been under the care of the Tashiro family, who have served as hereditary priests since its establishment. The shrine formerly maintained stables for horses, including retired racehorses such as {{ill|Basikeen|ja|バシケーン}} (winner of the 2010 Nakayama Grand Jump) and {{ill|Million Disc|ja|ミリオンディスク}} (winner of the 2009 Capella Stakes). However, the stables were dismantled around 2019.[https://news.netkeiba.com/?pid=news_view&no=72447 Basikeen Finds a New Home at Sōma Nakamura Shrine] == Cultural Properties == '''Important Cultural Properties''' * Main Shrine, Worship Hall, and Offering Hall (1 building) ''' Includes a "kuden" (宮殿) altar. == images == File:Soma otemon.JPG|{{ill|Nakamura Castle (Mutsu Province)|ja|中村城 (Mutsu Province )}} Otemon Gate (2013) File:Nakamura shrine 003.JPG|Entrance to Nakamura Shrine, featuring a horse statue associated with {{ill|Sōma Nomaoi|ja|相馬野馬追}} (2013) == See Also == * {{ill|Nakamura Castle (Mutsu Province)|ja|中村城 (Mutsu Province )}} * {{ill|Sōma Nomaoi|ja|相馬野馬追}} == External Links == * [https://soumanakamurajinja.com/ Sōma Nakamura Shrine Official Website] * revision.text:
{{infobox religious building | name = Sōma Nakamura Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Somanakamura shrine.jpg|270px]]
Haiden {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=270}} | location = 140 Nakamura, Kitamachi, Sōma City, Fukushima Prefecture, Japan | coordinates = {{coord|37|47|49.65|N|140|54|48.45|E|region:JP-07_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Sōma Nakamura Shrine}} | deity = [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]] | Shrine rank = | founded = 1611 (Keichō 16) | main hall = [[Nagare-zukuri]] | Other name = Myōken Nakamura Shrine | festival = April 18–19 | rituals = [[Sōma Nomaoi]] (July 23–25) | map = {{location map|Japan Fukushima Prefecture|label=Sōma Nakamura Shrine|float=center|relief=1|width=200|caption=}} }} '''Sōma Nakamura Shrine''' (相馬中村神社) is a Shinto shrine located in Nakamura, Sōma City, Fukushima Prefecture. It is also known as '''Myōken Nakamura Shrine''' (妙見中村神社) or simply Nakamura Shrine. The enshrined deity is {{ill|Ame-no-Minakanushi|ja|天之御中主神}} (identified with {{ill|Myōken Bosatsu|ja|妙見菩薩}}). The shrine serves as the starting point for the {{ill|Sōma Nomaoi|ja|相馬野馬追}} festival.{{Cite web|url=https://www.minyu-net.com/news/news/FM20211115-667668.php |title=Sōma Nakamura Shrine to Receive New Grand Torii After Earthquake Damage |publisher=Fukushima Minyu Newspaper |accessdate=2021-11-15}} == Overview == The origins of Sōma Nakamura Shrine trace back to the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}} when {{ill|Taira no Masakado|ja|Taira no Masakado|1=Taira no Masakado}}, the founder of the {{ill|Sōma clan|ja|相馬氏}}, established a Myōken Shrine in {{ill|Shimōsa Province|ja|下総国}} during the Jōhei era (931–937). As the Sōma clan moved to the Sōma district, a new shrine was established. By the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}} in the late 16th century, {{ill|Nakamura Castle (Mutsu Province)|ja|中村城 (Mutsu Province )}} became the clan's northern stronghold, governed by lords such as {{ill|Sōma Moritane|ja|相馬盛胤}} and {{ill|Sōma Takamine|ja|相馬隆胤}}. After the {{ill|Battle of Sekigahara|ja|関ヶ原の戦い}} in 1600, the Sōma clan was briefly dispossessed of their holdings but regained control in 1611, establishing the {{ill|Sōma Nakamura Domain|ja|相馬中村藩}}. The shrine was built by {{ill|Sōma Takatane|ja|相馬利胤}}, the first lord of the Nakamura Domain, near the southwest of Nakamura Castle in 1611. The current main shrine building was constructed in {{ill|1643|ja|寛永20th year }} by {{ill|Sōma Yoshitane|ja|相馬義胤 (相馬中村藩主)}}, the second domain lord, and has been designated as a {{ill|National Important Cultural Property|ja|重要文化財}}. During the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離}} led to the removal of Myōken Bosatsu, and the shrine was renamed Sōma Nakamura Shrine. Another shrine, {{ill|Sōma Shrine (Sōma City)|lt=Sōma Shrine|ja|相馬神社 (相馬市)}}, was established in 1880 on the ruins of Nakamura Castle's main keep after the {{ill|Boshin War|ja|戊辰戦争}}. The shrine has been under the care of the Tashiro family, who have served as hereditary priests since its establishment. The shrine formerly maintained stables for horses, including retired racehorses such as {{ill|Basikeen|ja|バシケーン}} (winner of the 2010 Nakayama Grand Jump) and {{ill|Million Disc|ja|ミリオンディスク}} (winner of the 2009 Capella Stakes). However, the stables were dismantled around 2019.[https://news.netkeiba.com/?pid=news_view&no=72447 Basikeen Finds a New Home at Sōma Nakamura Shrine] == Cultural Properties == '''Important Cultural Properties''' * Main Shrine, Worship Hall, and Offering Hall (1 building) ''' Includes a "kuden" (宮殿) altar. == images == File:Soma otemon.JPG|{{ill|Nakamura Castle (Mutsu Province)|ja|中村城 (Mutsu Province )}} Otemon Gate (2013) File:Nakamura shrine 003.JPG|Entrance to Nakamura Shrine, featuring a horse statue associated with {{ill|Sōma Nomaoi|ja|相馬野馬追}} (2013) == See Also == * {{ill|Nakamura Castle (Mutsu Province)|ja|中村城 (Mutsu Province )}} * {{ill|Sōma Nomaoi|ja|相馬野馬追}} == External Links == * [https://soumanakamurajinja.com/ Sōma Nakamura Shrine Official Website] * [https://ameblo.jp/umatoayumu-soma/ Uma to Ayumu SOMA Blog] ==References== {{reflist}} {{神道 横}} [[Category:Completion tier 2]][[Category: tier 2]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Sōma Nakamura {{infobox religious building | name = Sōma Nakamura Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Somanakamura shrine.jpg|270px]]
Haiden {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=270}} | location = 140 Nakamura, Kitamachi, Sōma City, Fukushima Prefecture, Japan | coordinates = {{coord|37|47|49.65|N|140|54|48.45|E|region:JP-07_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Sōma Nakamura Shrine}} | deity = [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]] | Shrine rank = | founded = 1611 (Keichō 16) | main hall = [[Nagare-zukuri]] | Other name = Myōken Nakamura Shrine | festival = April 18–19 | rituals = [[Sōma Nomaoi]] (July 23–25) | map = {{location map|Japan Fukushima Prefecture|label=Sōma Nakamura Shrine|float=center|relief=1|width=200|caption=}} }} '''Sōma Nakamura Shrine''' (相馬中村神社) is a Shinto shrine located in Nakamura, Sōma City, Fukushima Prefecture. It is also known as '''Myōken Nakamura Shrine''' (妙見中村神社) or simply Nakamura Shrine. The enshrined deity is {{ill|Ame-no-Minakanushi|ja|天之御中主神}} (identified with {{ill|Myōken Bosatsu|ja|妙見菩薩}}). The shrine serves as the starting point for the {{ill|Sōma Nomaoi|ja|相馬野馬追}} festival.{{Cite web|url=https://www.minyu-net.com/news/news/FM20211115-667668.php |title=Sōma Nakamura Shrine to Receive New Grand Torii After Earthquake Damage |publisher=Fukushima Minyu Newspaper |accessdate=2021-11-15}} == Overview == The origins of Sōma Nakamura Shrine trace back to the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}} when {{ill|Taira no Masakado|ja|Taira no Masakado|1=Taira no Masakado}}, the founder of the {{ill|Sōma clan|ja|相馬氏}}, established a Myōken Shrine in {{ill|Shimōsa Province|ja|下総国}} during the Jōhei era (931–937). As the Sōma clan moved to the Sōma district, a new shrine was established. By the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}} in the late 16th century, {{ill|Nakamura Castle (Mutsu Province)|ja|中村城 (Mutsu Province )}} became the clan's northern stronghold, governed by lords such as {{ill|Sōma Moritane|ja|相馬盛胤}} and {{ill|Sōma Takamine|ja|相馬隆胤}}. After the {{ill|Battle of Sekigahara|ja|関ヶ原の戦い}} in 1600, the Sōma clan was briefly dispossessed of their holdings but regained control in 1611, establishing the {{ill|Sōma Nakamura Domain|ja|相馬中村藩}}. The shrine was built by {{ill|Sōma Takatane|ja|相馬利胤}}, the first lord of the Nakamura Domain, near the southwest of Nakamura Castle in 1611. The current main shrine building was constructed in {{ill|1643|ja|寛永20th year }} by {{ill|Sōma Yoshitane|ja|相馬義胤 (相馬中村藩主)}}, the second domain lord, and has been designated as a {{ill|National Important Cultural Property|ja|重要文化財}}. During the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離}} led to the removal of Myōken Bosatsu, and the shrine was renamed Sōma Nakamura Shrine. Another shrine, {{ill|Sōma Shrine (Sōma City)|lt=Sōma Shrine|ja|相馬神社 (相馬市)}}, was established in 1880 on the ruins of Nakamura Castle's main keep after the {{ill|Boshin War|ja|戊辰戦争}}. The shrine has been under the care of the Tashiro family, who have served as hereditary priests since its establishment. The shrine formerly maintained stables for horses, including retired racehorses such as {{ill|Basikeen|ja|バシケーン}} (winner of the 2010 Nakayama Grand Jump) and {{ill|Million Disc|ja|ミリオンディスク}} (winner of the 2009 Capella Stakes). However, the stables were dismantled around 2019.[https://news.netkeiba.com/?pid=news_view&no=72447 Basikeen Finds a New Home at Sōma Nakamura Shrine] == Cultural Properties == '''Important Cultural Properties''' * Main Shrine, Worship Hall, and Offering Hall (1 building) ''' Includes a "kuden" (宮殿) altar. == images == File:Soma otemon.JPG|{{ill|Nakamura Castle (Mutsu Province)|ja|中村城 (Mutsu {{infobox religious building | name = Sōma Nakamura Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Somanakamura shrine.jpg|270px]]
Haiden {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=270}} | location = 140 Nakamura, Kitamachi, Sōma City, Fukushima Prefecture, Japan | coordinates = {{coord|37|47|49.65|N|140|54|48.45|E|region:JP-07_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Sōma Nakamura Shrine}} | deity = [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]] | Shrine rank = | founded = 1611 (Keichō 16) | main hall = [[Nagare-zukuri]] | Other name = Myōken Nakamura Shrine | festival = April 18–19 | rituals = [[Sōma Nomaoi]] (July 23–25) | map = {{location map|Japan Fukushima Prefecture|label=Sōma Nakamura Shrine|float=center|relief=1|width=200|caption=}} }} '''Sōma Nakamura Shrine''' (相馬中村神社) is a Shinto shrine located in Nakamura, Sōma City, Fukushima Prefecture. It is also known as '''Myōken Nakamura Shrine''' (妙見中村神社) or simply Nakamura Shrine. The enshrined deity is {{ill|Ame-no-Minakanushi|ja|天之御中主神}} (identified with {{ill|Myōken Bosatsu|ja|妙見菩薩}}). The shrine serves as the starting point for the {{ill|Sōma Nomaoi|ja|相馬野馬追}} festival.{{Cite web|url=https://www.minyu-net.com/news/news/FM20211115-667668.php |title=Sōma Nakamura Shrine to Receive New Grand Torii After Earthquake Damage |publisher=Fukushima Minyu Newspaper |accessdate=2021-11-15}} == Overview == The origins of Sōma Nakamura Shrine trace back to the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}} when {{ill|Taira no Masakado|ja|Taira no Masakado|1=Taira no Masakado}}, the founder of the {{ill|Sōma clan|ja|相馬氏}}, established a Myōken Shrine in {{ill|Shimōsa Province|ja|下総国}} during the Jōhei era (931–937). As the Sōma clan moved to the Sōma district, a new shrine was established. By the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}} in the late 16th century, {{ill|Nakamura Castle (Mutsu Province)|ja|中村城 (Mutsu Province )}} became the clan's northern stronghold, governed by lords such as {{ill|Sōma Moritane|ja|相馬盛胤}} and {{ill|Sōma Takamine|ja|相馬隆胤}}. After the {{ill|Battle of Sekigahara|ja|関ヶ原の戦い}} in 1600, the Sōma clan was briefly dispossessed of their holdings but regained control in 1611, establishing the {{ill|Sōma Nakamura Domain|ja|相馬中村藩}}. The shrine was built by {{ill|Sōma Takatane|ja|相馬利胤}}, the first lord of the Nakamura Domain, near the southwest of Nakamura Castle in 1611. The current main shrine building was constructed in {{ill|1643|ja|寛永20th year }} by {{ill|Sōma Yoshitane|ja|相馬義胤 (相馬中村藩主)}}, the second domain lord, and has been designated as a {{ill|National Important Cultural Property|ja|重要文化財}}. During the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離}} led to the removal of Myōken Bosatsu, and the shrine was renamed Sōma Nakamura Shrine. Another shrine, {{ill|Sōma Shrine (Sōma City)|lt=Sōma Shrine|ja|相馬神社 (相馬市)}}, was established in 1880 on the ruins of Nakamura Castle's main keep after the {{ill|Boshin War|ja|戊辰戦争}}. The shrine has been under the care of the Tashiro family, who have served as hereditary priests since its establishment. The shrine formerly maintained stables for horses, including retired racehorses such as {{ill|Basikeen|ja|バシケーン}} (winner of the 2010 Nakayama Grand Jump) and {{ill|Million Disc|ja|ミリオンディスク}} (winner of the 2009 Capella Stakes). However, the stables were dismantled around 2019.[https://news.netkeiba.com/?pid=news_view&no=72447 Basikeen Finds a New Home at Sōma Nakamura Shrine] == Cultural Properties == '''Important Cultural Properties''' * Main Shrine, Worship Hall, and Offering Hall (1 building) ''' Includes a "kuden" (宮殿) altar. == images == File:Soma otemon.JPG|{{ill|Nakamura Castle (Mutsu Province)|ja|中村城 (Mutsu Province )}} Otemon Gate (2013) File:Nakamura shrine 003.JPG|Entrance to Nakamura Shrine, featuring a horse statue associated with {{ill|Sōma Nomaoi|ja|相馬野馬追}} (2013) == See Also == * {{ill|Nakamura {{infobox religious building | name = Sōma Nakamura Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Somanakamura shrine.jpg|270px]]
Haiden {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=270}} | location = 140 Nakamura, Kitamachi, Sōma City, Fukushima Prefecture, Japan | coordinates = {{coord|37|47|49.65|N|140|54|48.45|E|region:JP-07_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Sōma Nakamura Shrine}} | deity = [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]] | Shrine rank = | founded = 1611 (Keichō 16) | main hall = [[Nagare-zukuri]] | Other name = Myōken Nakamura Shrine | festival = April 18–19 | rituals = [[Sōma Nomaoi]] (July 23–25) | map = {{location map|Japan Fukushima Prefecture|label=Sōma Nakamura Shrine|float=center|relief=1|width=200|caption=}} }} '''Sōma Nakamura Shrine''' (相馬中村神社) is a Shinto shrine located in Nakamura, Sōma City, Fukushima Prefecture. It is also known as '''Myōken Nakamura Shrine''' (妙見中村神社) or simply Nakamura Shrine. The enshrined deity is {{ill|Ame-no-Minakanushi|ja|天之御中主神}} (identified with {{ill|Myōken Bosatsu|ja|妙見菩薩}}). The shrine serves as the starting point for the {{ill|Sōma Nomaoi|ja|相馬野馬追}} festival.{{Cite web|url=https://www.minyu-net.com/news/news/FM20211115-667668.php |title=Sōma Nakamura Shrine to Receive New Grand Torii After Earthquake Damage |publisher=Fukushima Minyu Newspaper |accessdate=2021-11-15}} == Overview == The origins of Sōma Nakamura Shrine trace back to the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}} when {{ill|Taira no Masakado|ja|Taira no Masakado|1=Taira no Masakado}}, the founder of the {{ill|Sōma clan|ja|相馬氏}}, established a Myōken Shrine in {{ill|Shimōsa Province|ja|下総国}} during the Jōhei era (931–937). As the Sōma clan moved to the Sōma district, a new shrine was established. By the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}} in the late 16th century, {{ill|Nakamura Castle (Mutsu Province)|ja|中村城 (Mutsu Province )}} became the clan's northern stronghold, governed by lords such as {{ill|Sōma Moritane|ja|相馬盛胤}} and {{ill|Sōma Takamine|ja|相馬隆胤}}. After the {{ill|Battle of Sekigahara|ja|関ヶ原の戦い}} in 1600, the Sōma clan was briefly dispossessed of their holdings but regained control in 1611, establishing the {{ill|Sōma Nakamura Domain|ja|相馬中村藩}}. The shrine was built by {{ill|Sōma Takatane|ja|相馬利胤}}, the first lord of the Nakamura Domain, near the southwest of Nakamura Castle in 1611. The current main shrine building was constructed in {{ill|1643|ja|寛永20th year }} by {{ill|Sōma Yoshitane|ja|相馬義胤 (相馬中村藩主)}}, the second domain lord, and has been designated as a {{ill|National Important Cultural Property|ja|重要文化財}}. During the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離}} led to the removal of Myōken Bosatsu, and the shrine was renamed Sōma Nakamura Shrine. Another shrine, {{ill|Sōma Shrine (Sōma City)|lt=Sōma Shrine|ja|相馬神社 (相馬市)}}, was established in 1880 on the ruins of Nakamura Castle's main keep after the {{ill|Boshin War|ja|戊辰戦争}}. The shrine has been under the care of the Tashiro family, who have served as hereditary priests since its establishment. The shrine formerly maintained stables for horses, including retired racehorses such as {{ill|Basikeen|ja|バシケーン}} (winner of the 2010 Nakayama Grand Jump) and {{ill|Million Disc|ja|ミリオンディスク}} (winner of the 2009 Capella Stakes). However, the stables were dismantled around 2019.[https://news.netkeiba.com/?pid=news_view&no=72447 Basikeen Finds a New Home at Sōma Nakamura Shrine] == Cultural Properties == '''Important Cultural Properties''' * Main Shrine, Worship Hall, and Offering Hall (1 building) ''' Includes a "kuden" (宮殿) altar. == images == File:Soma otemon.JPG|{{ill|Nakamura Castle (Mutsu Province)|ja|中村城 (Mutsu Province )}} Otemon Gate (2013) File:Nakamura shrine 003.JPG|Entrance to Nakamura Shrine, featuring a horse statue associated with {{ill|Sōma Nomaoi|ja|相馬野馬追}} (2013) == See Also == * {{ill|Nakamura Castle (Mutsu Province)|ja|中村城 (Mutsu Province )}} * {{ill|Sōma Nomaoi|ja|相馬野馬追}} == External Links == * [https://soumanakamurajinja.com/ Sōma Nakamura Shrine Official Website] * {{infobox religious building | name = Sōma Nakamura Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Somanakamura shrine.jpg|270px]]
Haiden {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=270}} | location = 140 Nakamura, Kitamachi, Sōma City, Fukushima Prefecture, Japan | coordinates = {{coord|37|47|49.65|N|140|54|48.45|E|region:JP-07_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Sōma Nakamura Shrine}} | deity = [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]] | Shrine rank = | founded = 1611 (Keichō 16) | main hall = [[Nagare-zukuri]] | Other name = Myōken Nakamura Shrine | festival = April 18–19 | rituals = [[Sōma Nomaoi]] (July 23–25) | map = {{location map|Japan Fukushima Prefecture|label=Sōma Nakamura Shrine|float=center|relief=1|width=200|caption=}} }} '''Sōma Nakamura Shrine''' (相馬中村神社) is a Shinto shrine located in Nakamura, Sōma City, Fukushima Prefecture. It is also known as '''Myōken Nakamura Shrine''' (妙見中村神社) or simply Nakamura Shrine. The enshrined deity is {{ill|Ame-no-Minakanushi|ja|天之御中主神}} (identified with {{ill|Myōken Bosatsu|ja|妙見菩薩}}). The shrine serves as the starting point for the {{ill|Sōma Nomaoi|ja|相馬野馬追}} festival.{{Cite web|url=https://www.minyu-net.com/news/news/FM20211115-667668.php |title=Sōma Nakamura Shrine to Receive New Grand Torii After Earthquake Damage |publisher=Fukushima Minyu Newspaper |accessdate=2021-11-15}} == Overview == The origins of Sōma Nakamura Shrine trace back to the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}} when {{ill|Taira no Masakado|ja|Taira no Masakado|1=Taira no Masakado}}, the founder of the {{ill|Sōma clan|ja|相馬氏}}, established a Myōken Shrine in {{ill|Shimōsa Province|ja|下総国}} during the Jōhei era (931–937). As the Sōma clan moved to the Sōma district, a new shrine was established. By the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}} in the late 16th century, {{ill|Nakamura Castle (Mutsu Province)|ja|中村城 (Mutsu Province )}} became the clan's northern stronghold, governed by lords such as {{ill|Sōma Moritane|ja|相馬盛胤}} and {{ill|Sōma Takamine|ja|相馬隆胤}}. After the {{ill|Battle of Sekigahara|ja|関ヶ原の戦い}} in 1600, the Sōma clan was briefly dispossessed of their holdings but regained control in 1611, establishing the {{ill|Sōma Nakamura Domain|ja|相馬中村藩}}. The shrine was built by {{ill|Sōma Takatane|ja|相馬利胤}}, the first lord of the Nakamura Domain, near the southwest of Nakamura Castle in 1611. The current main shrine building was constructed in {{ill|1643|ja|寛永20th year }} by {{ill|Sōma Yoshitane|ja|相馬義胤 (相馬中村藩主)}}, the second domain lord, and has been designated as a {{ill|National Important Cultural Property|ja|重要文化財}}. During the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離}} led to the removal of Myōken Bosatsu, and the shrine was renamed Sōma Nakamura Shrine. Another shrine, {{ill|Sōma Shrine (Sōma City)|lt=Sōma Shrine|ja|相馬神社 (相馬市)}}, was established in 1880 on the ruins of Nakamura Castle's main keep after the {{ill|Boshin War|ja|戊辰戦争}}. The shrine has been under the care of the Tashiro family, who have served as hereditary priests since its establishment. The shrine formerly maintained stables for horses, including retired racehorses such as {{ill|Basikeen|ja|バシケーン}} (winner of the 2010 Nakayama Grand Jump) and {{ill|Million Disc|ja|ミリオンディスク}} (winner of the 2009 Capella Stakes). However, the stables were dismantled around 2019.[https://news.netkeiba.com/?pid=news_view&no=72447 Basikeen Finds a New Home at Sōma Nakamura Shrine] == Cultural Properties == '''Important Cultural Properties''' * Main Shrine, Worship Hall, and Offering Hall (1 building) ''' Includes a "kuden" (宮殿) altar. == images == File:Soma otemon.JPG|{{ill|Nakamura Castle (Mutsu Province)|ja|中村城 (Mutsu Province )}} Otemon Gate (2013) File:Nakamura shrine 003.JPG|Entrance to Nakamura Shrine, featuring a horse statue associated with {{ill|Sōma Nomaoi|ja|相馬野馬追}} (2013) == See Also == * {{ill|Nakamura Castle (Mutsu Province)|ja|中村城 (Mutsu Province )}} * {{ill|Sōma Nomaoi|ja|相馬野馬追}} == External Links == * [https://soumanakamurajinja.com/ Sōma Nakamura Shrine Official Website] * {{infobox religious building | name = Sōma Nakamura Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Somanakamura shrine.jpg|270px]]
Haiden {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=270}} | location = 140 Nakamura, Kitamachi, Sōma City, Fukushima Prefecture, Japan | coordinates = {{coord|37|47|49.65|N|140|54|48.45|E|region:JP-07_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Sōma Nakamura Shrine}} | deity = [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]] | Shrine rank = | founded = 1611 (Keichō 16) | main hall = [[Nagare-zukuri]] | Other name = Myōken Nakamura Shrine | festival = April 18–19 | rituals = [[Sōma Nomaoi]] (July 23–25) | map = {{location map|Japan Fukushima Prefecture|label=Sōma Nakamura Shrine|float=center|relief=1|width=200|caption=}} }} '''Sōma Nakamura Shrine''' (相馬中村神社) is a Shinto shrine located in Nakamura, Sōma City, Fukushima Prefecture. It is also known as '''Myōken Nakamura Shrine''' (妙見中村神社) or simply Nakamura Shrine. The enshrined deity is {{ill|Ame-no-Minakanushi|ja|天之御中主神}} (identified with {{ill|Myōken Bosatsu|ja|妙見菩薩}}). The shrine serves as the starting point for the {{ill|Sōma Nomaoi|ja|相馬野馬追}} festival.{{Cite web|url=https://www.minyu-net.com/news/news/FM20211115-667668.php |title=Sōma Nakamura Shrine to Receive New Grand Torii After Earthquake Damage |publisher=Fukushima Minyu Newspaper |accessdate=2021-11-15}} == Overview == The origins of Sōma Nakamura Shrine trace back to the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}} when {{ill|Taira no Masakado|ja|Taira no Masakado|1=Taira no Masakado}}, the founder of the {{ill|Sōma clan|ja|相馬氏}}, established a Myōken Shrine in {{ill|Shimōsa Province|ja|下総国}} during the Jōhei era (931–937). As the Sōma clan moved to the Sōma district, a new shrine was established. By the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}} in the late 16th century, {{ill|Nakamura Castle (Mutsu Province)|ja|中村城 (Mutsu Province )}} became the clan's northern stronghold, governed by lords such as {{ill|Sōma Moritane|ja|相馬盛胤}} and {{ill|Sōma Takamine|ja|相馬隆胤}}. After the {{ill|Battle of Sekigahara|ja|関ヶ原の戦い}} in 1600, the Sōma clan was briefly dispossessed of their holdings but regained control in 1611, establishing the {{ill|Sōma Nakamura Domain|ja|相馬中村藩}}. The shrine was built by {{ill|Sōma Takatane|ja|相馬利胤}}, the first lord of the Nakamura Domain, near the southwest of Nakamura Castle in 1611. The current main shrine building was constructed in {{ill|1643|ja|寛永20th year }} by {{ill|Sōma Yoshitane|ja|相馬義胤 (相馬中村藩主)}}, the second domain lord, and has been designated as a {{ill|National Important Cultural Property|ja|重要文化財}}. During the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離}} led to the removal of Myōken Bosatsu, and the shrine was renamed Sōma Nakamura Shrine. Another shrine, {{ill|Sōma Shrine (Sōma City)|lt=Sōma Shrine|ja|相馬神社 (相馬市)}}, was established in 1880 on the ruins of Nakamura Castle's main keep after the {{ill|Boshin War|ja|戊辰戦争}}. The shrine has been under the care of the Tashiro family, who have served as hereditary priests since its establishment. The shrine formerly maintained stables for horses, including retired racehorses such as {{ill|Basikeen|ja|バシケーン}} (winner of the 2010 Nakayama Grand Jump) and {{ill|Million Disc|ja|ミリオンディスク}} (winner of the 2009 Capella Stakes). However, the stables were dismantled around 2019.[https://news.netkeiba.com/?pid=news_view&no=72447 Basikeen Finds a New Home at Sōma Nakamura Shrine] == Cultural Properties == '''Important Cultural revision.comment: Bot: Reformatted page - moved categories, interwikis, DEFAULTSORT, and removed duplicates.
revision.text:
{{infobox religious building | name = Sōma Nakamura Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Somanakamura shrine.jpg|270px]]
Haiden {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=270}} | location = 140 Nakamura, Kitamachi, Sōma City, Fukushima Prefecture, Japan | coordinates = {{coord|37|47|49.65|N|140|54|48.45|E|region:JP-07_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Sōma Nakamura Shrine}} | deity = [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]] | Shrine rank = | founded = 1611 (Keichō 16) | main hall = [[Nagare-zukuri]] | Other name = Myōken Nakamura Shrine | festival = April 18–19 | rituals = [[Sōma Nomaoi]] (July 23–25) | map = {{location map|Japan Fukushima Prefecture|label=Sōma Nakamura Shrine|float=center|relief=1|width=200|caption=}} }} '''Sōma Nakamura Shrine''' (相馬中村神社) is a Shinto shrine located in Nakamura, Sōma City, Fukushima Prefecture. It is also known as '''Myōken Nakamura Shrine''' (妙見中村神社) or simply Nakamura Shrine. The enshrined deity is {{ill|Ame-no-Minakanushi|ja|天之御中主神}} (identified with {{ill|Myōken Bosatsu|ja|妙見菩薩}}). The shrine serves as the starting point for the {{ill|Sōma Nomaoi|ja|相馬野馬追}} festival.{{Cite web|url=https://www.minyu-net.com/news/news/FM20211115-667668.php |title=Sōma Nakamura Shrine to Receive New Grand Torii After Earthquake Damage |publisher=Fukushima Minyu Newspaper |accessdate=2021-11-15}} == Overview == The origins of Sōma Nakamura Shrine trace back to the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}} when {{ill|Taira no Masakado|ja|Taira no Masakado|1=Taira no Masakado}}, the founder of the {{ill|Sōma clan|ja|相馬氏}}, established a Myōken Shrine in {{ill|Shimōsa Province|ja|下総国}} during the Jōhei era (931–937). As the Sōma clan moved to the Sōma district, a new shrine was established. By the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}} in the late 16th century, {{ill|Nakamura Castle (Mutsu Province)|ja|中村城 (Mutsu Province )}} became the clan's northern stronghold, governed by lords such as {{ill|Sōma Moritane|ja|相馬盛胤}} and {{ill|Sōma Takamine|ja|相馬隆胤}}. After the {{ill|Battle of Sekigahara|ja|関ヶ原の戦い}} in 1600, the Sōma clan was briefly dispossessed of their holdings but regained control in 1611, establishing the {{ill|Sōma Nakamura Domain|ja|相馬中村藩}}. The shrine was built by {{ill|Sōma Takatane|ja|相馬利胤}}, the first lord of the Nakamura Domain, near the southwest of Nakamura Castle in 1611. The current main shrine building was constructed in {{ill|1643|ja|寛永20th year }} by {{ill|Sōma Yoshitane|ja|相馬義胤 (相馬中村藩主)}}, the second domain lord, and has been designated as a {{ill|National Important Cultural Property|ja|重要文化財}}. During the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離}} led to the removal of Myōken Bosatsu, and the shrine was renamed Sōma Nakamura Shrine. Another shrine, {{ill|Sōma Shrine (Sōma City)|lt=Sōma Shrine|ja|相馬神社 (相馬市)}}, was established in 1880 on the ruins of Nakamura Castle's main keep after the {{ill|Boshin War|ja|戊辰戦争}}. The shrine has been under the care of the Tashiro family, who have served as hereditary priests since its establishment. The shrine formerly maintained stables for horses, including retired racehorses such as {{ill|Basikeen|ja|バシケーン}} (winner of the 2010 Nakayama Grand Jump) and {{ill|Million Disc|ja|ミリオンディスク}} (winner of the 2009 Capella Stakes). However, the stables were dismantled around 2019.[https://news.netkeiba.com/?pid=news_view&no=72447 Basikeen Finds a New Home at Sōma Nakamura Shrine] == Cultural Properties == '''Important Cultural Properties''' * Main Shrine, Worship Hall, and Offering Hall (1 building) ''' Includes a "kuden" (宮殿) altar. == images == File:Soma otemon.JPG|{{ill|Nakamura Castle (Mutsu Province)|ja|中村城 (Mutsu Province )}} Otemon Gate (2013) File:Nakamura shrine 003.JPG|Entrance to Nakamura Shrine, featuring a horse statue associated with {{ill|Sōma Nomaoi|ja|相馬野馬追}} (2013) == See Also == * {{ill|Nakamura Castle (Mutsu Province)|ja|中村城 (Mutsu Province )}} * {{ill|Sōma Nomaoi|ja|相馬野馬追}} == External Links == * [https://soumanakamurajinja.com/ Sōma Nakamura Shrine Official Website] * [https://ameblo.jp/umatoayumu-soma/ Uma to Ayumu SOMA Blog] ==References== {{reflist}} {{神道 横}} {{Myōken Faith}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Sōma Nakamura Shrine]] [[Category:Shrines in Fukushima Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Sōma City]] [[Category:History of Sōma City]] [[Category:Edo-period architecture]] [[Category:Sōma clan]] [[Category:Sōma Shrine]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Fukushima Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties (buildings)]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Sōma, Fukushima]] [[Category:History of Sōma, Fukushima]] [[Category:Sōma Shrines]] [[Category:tab went inactive so I need to add another category]] [[Category:bump1-19]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] Properties''' * Main Shrine, Worship Hall, and Offering Hall (1 building) ''' Includes a "kuden" (宮殿) altar. == images == File:Soma otemon.JPG|{{ill|Nakamura Castle (Mutsu Province)|ja|中村城 (Mutsu revision.comment: Text replacement - "↵↵↵↵" to " "
revision.text:
{{infobox religious building | name = Sōma Nakamura Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Somanakamura shrine.jpg|270px]]
Haiden {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=270}} | location = 140 Nakamura, Kitamachi, Sōma City, Fukushima Prefecture, Japan | coordinates = {{coord|37|47|49.65|N|140|54|48.45|E|region:JP-07_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Sōma Nakamura Shrine}} | deity = [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]] | Shrine rank = | founded = 1611 (Keichō 16) | main hall = [[Nagare-zukuri]] | Other name = Myōken Nakamura Shrine | festival = April 18–19 | rituals = [[Sōma Nomaoi]] (July 23–25) | map = {{location map|Japan Fukushima Prefecture|label=Sōma Nakamura Shrine|float=center|relief=1|width=200|caption=}} }} '''Sōma Nakamura Shrine''' (相馬中村神社) is a Shinto shrine located in Nakamura, Sōma City, Fukushima Prefecture. It is also known as '''Myōken Nakamura Shrine''' (妙見中村神社) or simply Nakamura Shrine. The enshrined deity is {{ill|Ame-no-Minakanushi|ja|天之御中主神|12=draft}} (identified with {{ill|Myōken Bosatsu|ja|妙見菩薩|12=draft}}). The shrine serves as the starting point for the {{ill|Sōma Nomaoi|ja|相馬野馬追|12=draft}} festival.{{Cite web|url=https://www.minyu-net.com/news/news/FM20211115-667668.php |title=Sōma Nakamura Shrine to Receive New Grand Torii After Earthquake Damage |publisher=Fukushima Minyu Newspaper |accessdate=2021-11-15}} == Overview == The origins of Sōma Nakamura Shrine trace back to the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代|12=draft}} when {{ill|Taira no Masakado|ja|Taira no Masakado|1=Taira no Masakado|12=draft}}, the founder of the {{ill|Sōma clan|ja|相馬氏|12=draft}}, established a Myōken Shrine in {{ill|Shimōsa Province|ja|下総国|12=draft}} during the Jōhei era (931–937). As the Sōma clan moved to the Sōma district, a new shrine was established. By the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|12=draft}} in the late 16th century, {{ill|Nakamura Castle (Mutsu Province)|ja|中村城 (Mutsu Province )|12=draft}} became the clan's northern stronghold, governed by lords such as {{ill|Sōma Moritane|ja|相馬盛胤|12=draft}} and {{ill|Sōma Takamine|ja|相馬隆胤|12=draft}}. After the {{ill|Battle of Sekigahara|ja|関ヶ原の戦い|12=draft}} in 1600, the Sōma clan was briefly dispossessed of their holdings but regained control in 1611, establishing the {{ill|Sōma Nakamura Domain|ja|相馬中村藩|12=draft}}. The shrine was built by {{ill|Sōma Takatane|ja|相馬利胤|12=draft}}, the first lord of the Nakamura Domain, near the southwest of Nakamura Castle in 1611. The current main shrine building was constructed in {{ill|1643|ja|寛永20th year|12=draft}} by {{ill|Sōma Yoshitane|ja|相馬義胤 (相馬中村藩主)|12=draft}}, the second domain lord, and has been designated as a {{ill|National Important Cultural Property|ja|重要文化財|12=draft}}. During the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新|12=draft}}, {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離|12=draft}} led to the removal of Myōken Bosatsu, and the shrine was renamed Sōma Nakamura Shrine. Another shrine, {{ill|Sōma Shrine (Sōma City)|lt=Sōma Shrine|ja|相馬神社 (相馬市)|12=draft}}, was established in 1880 on the ruins of Nakamura Castle's main keep after the {{ill|Boshin War|ja|戊辰戦争|12=draft}}. The shrine has been under the care of the Tashiro family, who have served as hereditary priests since its establishment. The shrine formerly maintained stables for horses, including retired racehorses such as {{ill|Basikeen|ja|バシケーン|12=draft}} (winner of the 2010 Nakayama Grand Jump) and {{ill|Million Disc|ja|ミリオンディスク|12=draft}} (winner of the 2009 Capella Stakes). However, the stables were dismantled around 2019.[https://news.netkeiba.com/?pid=news_view&no=72447 Basikeen Finds a New Home at Sōma Nakamura Shrine] == Cultural Properties == '''Important Cultural Properties''' * Main Shrine, Worship Hall, and Offering Hall (1 building) ''' Includes a "kuden" (宮殿) altar. == images == File:Soma otemon.JPG|{{ill|Nakamura Castle (Mutsu Province)|ja|中村城 (Mutsu Province )|12=draft}} Otemon Gate (2013) File:Nakamura shrine 003.JPG|Entrance to Nakamura Shrine, featuring a horse statue associated with {{ill|Sōma Nomaoi|ja|相馬野馬追|12=draft}} (2013) == See Also == * {{ill|Nakamura Castle (Mutsu Province)|ja|中村城 (Mutsu Province )|12=draft}} * {{ill|Sōma Nomaoi|ja|相馬野馬追|12=draft}} == External Links == * [https://soumanakamurajinja.com/ Sōma Nakamura Shrine Official Website] * [https://ameblo.jp/umatoayumu-soma/ Uma to Ayumu SOMA Blog] ==References== {{reflist}} {{神道 横}} {{Myōken Faith}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:qq]] However, the stables were dismantled around 2019.[https://news.netkeiba.com/?pid=news_view&no=72447 Basikeen Finds a New Home at Sōma Nakamura Shrine] == Cultural Properties == '''Important Cultural Properties''' * Main Shrine, Worship Hall, and Offering Hall (1 building) ''' Includes a "kuden" (宮殿) altar. == images == File:Soma otemon.JPG|{{ill|Nakamura Castle (Mutsu Province)|ja|中村城 (Mutsu Province )|12=draft}} Otemon Gate (2013) File:Nakamura shrine 003.JPG|Entrance to Nakamura Shrine, featuring a horse statue associated with {{ill|Sōma Nomaoi|ja|相馬野馬追|12=draft}} (2013) == See Also == * {{ill|Nakamura Castle (Mutsu Province)|ja|中村城 (Mutsu Province )|12=draft}} * {{ill|Sōma Nomaoi|ja|相馬野馬追|12=draft}} == External Links == * [https://soumanakamurajinja.com/ Sōma Nakamura Shrine Official Website] * [https://ameblo.jp/umatoayumu-soma/ Uma to Ayumu SOMA Blog] ==References== {{reflist}} {{神道 横}} {{Myōken Faith}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Sōma Nakamura Shrine]] [[Category:Shrines in Fukushima Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Sōma City]] [[Category:History of Sōma City]] [[Category:Edo-period architecture]] [[Category:Sōma clan]] [[Category:Sōma Shrine]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Fukushima Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties (buildings)]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Sōma, Fukushima]] [[Category:History of Sōma, Fukushima]] [[Category:Sōma Shrines]] [[Category:tab went inactive so I need to add another category]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New4]] [[Category:pages which might possibly be incomplete but are most likely complete]] }} [[sn:Sōma Nakamura Shrine]] [[ja:相馬中村神社]] [[en:Sōma Nakamura Shrine]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Sōma Nakamura Shrine}}

revision.text:
{{infobox religious building | name = Sōma Nakamura Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Somanakamura shrine.jpg|270px]]
Haiden {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=270}} | location = 140 Nakamura, Kitamachi, Sōma City, Fukushima Prefecture, Japan | coordinates = {{coord|37|47|49.65|N|140|54|48.45|E|region:JP-07_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Sōma Nakamura Shrine}} | deity = [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]] | Shrine rank = | founded = 1611 (Keichō 16) | main hall = [[Nagare-zukuri]] | Other name = Myōken Nakamura Shrine | festival = April 18–19 | rituals = [[Sōma Nomaoi]] (July 23–25) | map = {{location map|Japan Fukushima Prefecture|label=Sōma Nakamura Shrine|float=center|relief=1|width=200|caption=}} }} '''Sōma Nakamura Shrine''' (相馬中村神社) is a Shinto shrine located in Nakamura, Sōma City, Fukushima Prefecture. It is also known as '''Myōken Nakamura Shrine''' (妙見中村神社) or simply Nakamura Shrine. The enshrined deity is {{ill|Ame-no-Minakanushi|ja|天之御中主神|12=draft}} (identified with {{ill|Myōken Bosatsu|ja|妙見菩薩|12=draft}}). The shrine serves as the starting point for the {{ill|Sōma Nomaoi|ja|相馬野馬追|12=draft}} festival.{{Cite web|url=https://www.minyu-net.com/news/news/FM20211115-667668.php |title=Sōma Nakamura Shrine to Receive New Grand Torii After Earthquake Damage |publisher=Fukushima Minyu Newspaper |accessdate=2021-11-15}} == Overview == The origins of Sōma Nakamura Shrine trace back to the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代|12=draft}} when {{ill|Taira no Masakado|ja|Taira no Masakado|1=Taira no Masakado|12=draft}}, the founder of the {{ill|Sōma clan|ja|相馬氏|12=draft}}, established a Myōken Shrine in {{ill|Shimōsa Province|ja|下総国|12=draft}} during the Jōhei era (931–937). As the Sōma clan moved to the Sōma district, a new shrine was established. By the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|12=draft}} in the late 16th century, {{ill|Nakamura Castle (Mutsu Province)|ja|中村城 (Mutsu Province )|12=draft}} became the clan's northern stronghold, governed by lords such as {{ill|Sōma Moritane|ja|相馬盛胤|12=draft}} and {{ill|Sōma Takamine|ja|相馬隆胤|12=draft}}. After the {{ill|Battle of Sekigahara|ja|関ヶ原の戦い|12=draft}} in 1600, the Sōma clan was briefly dispossessed of their holdings but regained control in 1611, establishing the {{ill|Sōma Nakamura Domain|ja|相馬中村藩|12=draft}}. The shrine was built by {{ill|Sōma Takatane|ja|相馬利胤|12=draft}}, the first lord of the Nakamura Domain, near the southwest of Nakamura Castle in 1611. The current main shrine building was constructed in {{ill|1643|ja|寛永20th year|12=draft}} by {{ill|Sōma Yoshitane|ja|相馬義胤 (相馬中村藩主)|12=draft}}, the second domain lord, and has been designated as a {{ill|National Important Cultural Property|ja|重要文化財|12=draft}}. During the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新|12=draft}}, {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離|12=draft}} led to the removal of Myōken Bosatsu, and the shrine was renamed Sōma Nakamura Shrine. Another shrine, {{ill|Sōma Shrine (Sōma City)|lt=Sōma Shrine|ja|相馬神社 (相馬市)|12=draft}}, was established in 1880 on the ruins of Nakamura Castle's main keep after the {{ill|Boshin War|ja|戊辰戦争|12=draft}}. The shrine has been under the care of the Tashiro family, who have served as hereditary priests since its establishment. The shrine formerly maintained stables for horses, including retired racehorses such as {{ill|Basikeen|ja|バシケーン|12=draft}} (winner of the 2010 Nakayama Grand Jump) and {{ill|Million Disc|ja|ミリオンディスク|12=draft}} (winner of the 2009 Capella Stakes). However, the stables were dismantled around 2019.[https://news.netkeiba.com/?pid=news_view&no=72447 Basikeen Finds a New Home at Sōma Nakamura Shrine] == Cultural Properties == '''Important Cultural Properties''' * Main Shrine, Worship Hall, and Offering Hall (1 building) ''' Includes a "kuden" (宮殿) altar. == images == File:Soma otemon.JPG|{{ill|Nakamura Castle (Mutsu Province)|ja|中村城 (Mutsu Province )|12=draft}} Otemon Gate (2013) File:Nakamura shrine 003.JPG|Entrance to Nakamura Shrine, featuring a horse statue associated with {{ill|Sōma Nomaoi|ja|相馬野馬追|12=draft}} (2013) == See Also == * {{ill|Nakamura Castle (Mutsu Province)|ja|中村城 (Mutsu Province )|12=draft}} * {{ill|Sōma Nomaoi|ja|相馬野馬追|12=draft}} == External Links == * [https://soumanakamurajinja.com/ Sōma Nakamura Shrine Official Website] * [https://ameblo.jp/umatoayumu-soma/ Uma to Ayumu SOMA Blog] ==References== {{reflist}} {{神道 横}} {{Myōken Faith}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Sōma Nakamura Shrine]] [[Category:Shrines in Fukushima Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Sōma City]] [[Category:History of Sōma City]] [[Category:Edo-period architecture]] [[Category:Sōma clan]] [[Category:Sōma Shrine]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Fukushima Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties (buildings)]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Sōma, Fukushima]] [[Category:History of Sōma, Fukushima]] [[Category:Sōma Shrines]] [[Category:tab went inactive so I need to add another category]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:pages which might possibly be incomplete but are most likely complete]] }} [[sn:Sōma Nakamura Shrine]] [[ja:相馬中村神社]] [[en:Sōma Nakamura Shrine]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Sōma Nakamura Shrine}}

However, the stables were dismantled around 2019.[https://news.netkeiba.com/?pid=news_view&no=72447 Basikeen Finds a New Home at Sōma Nakamura Shrine] == Cultural Properties == '''Important Cultural Properties''' * Main Shrine, Worship Hall, and Offering Hall (1 building) ''' Includes a "kuden" (宮殿) altar. == images == File:Soma otemon.JPG|{{ill|Nakamura Castle (Mutsu Province)|ja|中村城 (Mutsu Province )|12=draft}} Otemon Gate (2013) File:Nakamura shrine 003.JPG|Entrance to Nakamura Shrine, featuring a horse statue associated with {{ill|Sōma Nomaoi|ja|相馬野馬追|12=draft}} (2013) == See Also == * {{ill|Nakamura Castle (Mutsu Province)|ja|中村城 (Mutsu Province )|12=draft}} * {{ill|Sōma Nomaoi|ja|相馬野馬追|12=draft}} == External Links == * [https://soumanakamurajinja.com/ Sōma Nakamura Shrine Official Website] * [https://ameblo.jp/umatoayumu-soma/ Uma to Ayumu SOMA Blog] ==References== {{reflist}} {{神道 横}} {{Myōken Faith}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Sōma Nakamura Shrine]] [[Category:Shrines in Fukushima Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Sōma City]] [[Category:History of Sōma City]] [[Category:Edo-period architecture]] [[Category:Sōma clan]] [[Category:Sōma Shrine]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Fukushima Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties (buildings)]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Sōma, Fukushima]] [[Category:History of Sōma, Fukushima]] [[Category:Sōma Shrines]] [[Category:tab went inactive so I need to add another category]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:New]] }} [[sn:Sōma Nakamura Shrine]] [[ja:相馬中村神社]] [[en:Sōma Nakamura Shrine]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Sōma Nakamura Shrine}}

revision.comment: Text replacement - "|重要文化財|" to "|重要文化財|1=Important Cultural Property (Japan)|2=ja|3=重要文化財|lt=Important Cultural Property|qq= (Japan)|4=ko|5=일본의_중요문화재|"
revision.text:
{{infobox religious building | name = Sōma Nakamura Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Somanakamura shrine.jpg|270px]]
Haiden {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=270}} | location = 140 Nakamura, Kitamachi, Sōma City, Fukushima Prefecture, Japan | coordinates = {{coord|37|47|49.65|N|140|54|48.45|E|region:JP-07_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Sōma Nakamura Shrine}} | deity = [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]] | Shrine rank = | founded = 1611 (Keichō 16) | main hall = [[Nagare-zukuri]] | Other name = Myōken Nakamura Shrine | festival = April 18–19 | rituals = [[Sōma Nomaoi]] (July 23–25) | map = {{location map|Japan Fukushima Prefecture|label=Sōma Nakamura Shrine|float=center|relief=1|width=200|caption=}} }} '''Sōma Nakamura Shrine''' (相馬中村神社) is a Shinto shrine located in Nakamura, Sōma City, Fukushima Prefecture. It is also known as '''Myōken Nakamura Shrine''' (妙見中村神社) or simply Nakamura Shrine. The enshrined deity is {{ill|Ame-no-Minakanushi|ja|天之御中主神|12=draft}} (identified with {{ill|Myōken Bosatsu|ja|妙見菩薩|12=draft}}). The shrine serves as the starting point for the {{ill|Sōma Nomaoi|ja|相馬野馬追|12=draft}} festival.{{Cite web|url=https://www.minyu-net.com/news/news/FM20211115-667668.php |title=Sōma Nakamura Shrine to Receive New Grand Torii After Earthquake Damage |publisher=Fukushima Minyu Newspaper |accessdate=2021-11-15}} == Overview == The origins of Sōma Nakamura Shrine trace back to the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代|12=draft}} when {{ill|Taira no Masakado|ja|Taira no Masakado|1=Taira no Masakado|12=draft}}, the founder of the {{ill|Sōma clan|ja|相馬氏|12=draft}}, established a Myōken Shrine in {{ill|Shimōsa Province|ja|下総国|12=draft}} during the Jōhei era (931–937). As the Sōma clan moved to the Sōma district, a new shrine was established. By the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|12=draft}} in the late 16th century, {{ill|Nakamura Castle (Mutsu Province)|ja|中村城 (Mutsu Province )|12=draft}} became the clan's northern stronghold, governed by lords such as {{ill|Sōma Moritane|ja|相馬盛胤|12=draft}} and {{ill|Sōma Takamine|ja|相馬隆胤|12=draft}}. After the {{ill|Battle of Sekigahara|ja|関ヶ原の戦い|12=draft}} in 1600, the Sōma clan was briefly dispossessed of their holdings but regained control in 1611, establishing the {{ill|Sōma Nakamura Domain|ja|相馬中村藩|12=draft}}. The shrine was built by {{ill|Sōma Takatane|ja|相馬利胤|12=draft}}, the first lord of the Nakamura Domain, near the southwest of Nakamura Castle in 1611. The current main shrine building was constructed in {{ill|1643|ja|寛永20th year|12=draft}} by {{ill|Sōma Yoshitane|ja|相馬義胤 (相馬中村藩主)|12=draft}}, the second domain lord, and has been designated as a {{ill|National Important Cultural Property|ja|重要文化財|1=Important Cultural Property (Japan)|2=ja|3=重要文化財|lt=Important Cultural Property|qq= (Japan)|4=ko|5=일본의_중요문화재|12=draft}}. During the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新|12=draft}}, {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離|12=draft}} led to the removal of Myōken Bosatsu, and the shrine was renamed Sōma Nakamura Shrine. Another shrine, {{ill|Sōma Shrine (Sōma City)|lt=Sōma Shrine|ja|相馬神社 (相馬市)|12=draft}}, was established in 1880 on the ruins of Nakamura Castle's main keep after the {{ill|Boshin War|ja|戊辰戦争|12=draft}}. The shrine has been under the care of the Tashiro family, who have served as hereditary priests since its establishment. The shrine formerly maintained stables for horses, including retired racehorses such as {{ill|Basikeen|ja|バシケーン|12=draft}} (winner of the 2010 Nakayama Grand Jump) and {{ill|Million Disc|ja|ミリオンディスク|12=draft}} (winner of the 2009 Capella Stakes). However, the stables were dismantled around 2019.[https://news.netkeiba.com/?pid=news_view&no=72447 Basikeen Finds a New Home at Sōma Nakamura Shrine] == Cultural Properties == '''Important Cultural Properties''' * Main Shrine, Worship Hall, and Offering Hall (1 building) ''' Includes a "kuden" (宮殿) altar. == images == File:Soma otemon.JPG|{{ill|Nakamura Castle (Mutsu Province)|ja|中村城 (Mutsu Province )|12=draft}} Otemon Gate (2013) File:Nakamura shrine 003.JPG|Entrance to Nakamura Shrine, featuring a horse statue associated with {{ill|Sōma Nomaoi|ja|相馬野馬追|12=draft}} (2013) == See Also == * {{ill|Nakamura Castle (Mutsu Province)|ja|中村城 (Mutsu Province )|12=draft}} * {{ill|Sōma Nomaoi|ja|相馬野馬追|12=draft}} == External Links == * [https://soumanakamurajinja.com/ Sōma Nakamura Shrine Official Website] * [https://ameblo.jp/umatoayumu-soma/ Uma to Ayumu SOMA Blog] ==References== {{reflist}} {{神道 横}} {{Myōken Faith}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Sōma Nakamura Shrine]] [[Category:Shrines in Fukushima Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Sōma City]] [[Category:History of Sōma City]] [[Category:Edo-period architecture]] [[Category:Sōma clan]] [[Category:Sōma Shrine]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Fukushima Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties (buildings)]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Sōma, Fukushima]] [[Category:History of Sōma, Fukushima]] [[Category:Sōma Shrines]] [[Category:tab went inactive so I need to add another category]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:New]] }} [[sn:Sōma Nakamura Shrine]] [[ja:相馬中村神社]] [[en:Sōma Nakamura Shrine]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Sōma Nakamura Shrine}}

Another shrine, {{ill|Sōma Shrine (Sōma City)|lt=Sōma Shrine|ja|相馬神社 (相馬市)|12=draft|12=draft}}, was established in 1880 on the ruins of Nakamura Castle's main keep after the {{ill|Boshin War|ja|戊辰戦争|12=draft|12=draft}}. The shrine has been under the care of the Tashiro family, who have served as hereditary priests since its establishment. The shrine formerly maintained stables for horses, including retired racehorses such as {{ill|Basikeen|ja|バシケーン|12=draft|12=draft}} (winner of the 2010 Nakayama Grand Jump) and {{ill|Million Disc|ja|ミリオンディスク|12=draft|12=draft}} (winner of the 2009 Capella Stakes). However, the stables were dismantled around 2019.[https://news.netkeiba.com/?pid=news_view&no=72447 Basikeen Finds a New Home at Sōma Nakamura Shrine] == Cultural Properties == '''Important Cultural Properties''' * Main Shrine, Worship Hall, and Offering Hall (1 building) ''' Includes a "kuden" (宮殿) altar. == images == File:Soma otemon.JPG|{{ill|Nakamura Castle (Mutsu Province)|ja|中村城 (Mutsu Province )|12=draft|12=draft}} Otemon Gate (2013) File:Nakamura shrine 003.JPG|Entrance to Nakamura Shrine, featuring a horse statue associated with {{ill|Sōma Nomaoi|ja|相馬野馬追|12=draft|12=draft}} (2013) == See Also == * {{ill|Nakamura Castle (Mutsu Province)|ja|中村城 (Mutsu Province )|12=draft|12=draft}} * {{ill|Sōma Nomaoi|ja|相馬野馬追|12=draft|12=draft}} == External Links == * [https://soumanakamurajinja.com/ Sōma Nakamura Shrine Official Website] * [https://ameblo.jp/umatoayumu-soma/ Uma to Ayumu SOMA Blog] ==References== {{reflist}} {{神道 横}} {{Myōken Faith}} {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Sōma Nakamura Shrine]] [[Category:Shrines in Fukushima Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Sōma City]] [[Category:History of Sōma City]] [[Category:Edo-period architecture]] [[Category:Sōma clan]] [[Category:Sōma Shrine]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Fukushima Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties (buildings)]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Sōma, Fukushima]] [[Category:History of Sōma, Fukushima]] [[Category:Sōma Shrines]] [[Category:tab went inactive so I need to add another category]] [[Category:New]] }} [[sn:Sōma Nakamura Shrine]] [[ja:相馬中村神社]] [[en:Sōma Nakamura Shrine]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Sōma Nakamura Shrine}}

revision.text:
{{infobox religious building | name = Sōma Nakamura Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Somanakamura shrine.jpg|270px]]
Haiden {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=270}} | location = 140 Nakamura, Kitamachi, Sōma City, Fukushima Prefecture, Japan | coordinates = {{coord|37|47|49.65|N|140|54|48.45|E|region:JP-07_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Sōma Nakamura Shrine}} | deity = [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]] | Shrine Rank = | founded = 1611 (Keichō 16) | main hall = [[Nagare-zukuri]] | Other name = Myōken Nakamura Shrine | festival = April 18–19 | rituals = [[Sōma Nomaoi]] (July 23–25) | map = {{location map|Japan Fukushima Prefecture|label=Sōma Nakamura Shrine|float=center|relief=1|width=200|caption=}} }} '''Sōma Nakamura Shrine''' (相馬中村神社) is a Shinto shrine located in Nakamura, Sōma City, Fukushima Prefecture. It is also known as '''Myōken Nakamura Shrine''' (妙見中村神社) or simply Nakamura Shrine. The enshrined deity is {{ill|Ame-no-Minakanushi|ja|天之御中主神|12=draft|12=draft}} (identified with {{ill|Myōken Bosatsu|ja|妙見菩薩|12=draft|12=draft}}). The shrine serves as the starting point for the {{ill|Sōma Nomaoi|ja|相馬野馬追|12=draft|12=draft}} festival.{{Cite web|url=https://www.minyu-net.com/news/news/FM20211115-667668.php |title=Sōma Nakamura Shrine to Receive New Grand Torii After Earthquake Damage |publisher=Fukushima Minyu Newspaper |accessdate=2021-11-15}} == Overview == The origins of Sōma Nakamura Shrine trace back to the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代|12=draft|12=draft}} when {{ill|Taira no Masakado|ja|Taira no Masakado|1=Taira no Masakado|12=draft|12=draft}}, the founder of the {{ill|Sōma clan|ja|相馬氏|12=draft|12=draft}}, established a Myōken Shrine in {{ill|Shimōsa Province|ja|下総国|12=draft|12=draft}} during the Jōhei era (931–937). As the Sōma clan moved to the Sōma district, a new shrine was established. By the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|12=draft|12=draft}} in the late 16th century, {{ill|Nakamura Castle (Mutsu Province)|ja|中村城 (Mutsu Province )|12=draft|12=draft}} became the clan's northern stronghold, governed by lords such as {{ill|Sōma Moritane|ja|相馬盛胤|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Sōma Takamine|ja|相馬隆胤|12=draft|12=draft}}. After the {{ill|Battle of Sekigahara|ja|関ヶ原の戦い|12=draft|12=draft}} in 1600, the Sōma clan was briefly dispossessed of their holdings but regained control in 1611, establishing the {{ill|Sōma Nakamura Domain|ja|相馬中村藩|12=draft|12=draft}}. The shrine was built by {{ill|Sōma Takatane|ja|相馬利胤|12=draft|12=draft}}, the first lord of the Nakamura Domain, near the southwest of Nakamura Castle in 1611. The current main shrine building was constructed in {{ill|1643|ja|寛永20th year|12=draft|12=draft}} by {{ill|Sōma Yoshitane|ja|相馬義胤 (相馬中村藩主)|12=draft|12=draft}}, the second domain lord, and has been designated as a {{ill|National Important Cultural Property|ja|重要文化財|1=Important Cultural Property (Japan)|2=ja|3=重要文化財|lt=Important Cultural Property|qq= (Japan)|4=ko|5=일본의_중요문화재|12=draft|12=draft}}. During the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離|12=draft|12=draft}} led to the removal of Myōken Bosatsu, and the shrine was renamed Sōma Nakamura Shrine. Another shrine, {{ill|Sōma Shrine (Sōma City)|lt=Sōma Shrine|ja|相馬神社 (相馬市)|12=draft|12=draft}}, was established in 1880 on the ruins of Nakamura Castle's main keep after the {{ill|Boshin War|ja|戊辰戦争|12=draft|12=draft}}. The shrine has been under the care of the Tashiro family, who have served as hereditary priests since its establishment. The shrine formerly maintained stables for horses, including retired racehorses such as {{ill|Basikeen|ja|バシケーン|12=draft|12=draft}} (winner of the 2010 Nakayama Grand Jump) and {{ill|Million Disc|ja|ミリオンディスク|12=draft|12=draft}} (winner of the 2009 Capella Stakes). However, the stables were dismantled around 2019.[https://news.netkeiba.com/?pid=news_view&no=72447 Basikeen Finds a New Home at Sōma Nakamura Shrine] == Cultural Properties == '''Important Cultural Properties''' * Main Shrine, Worship Hall, and Offering Hall (1 building) ''' Includes a "kuden" (宮殿) altar. == images == File:Soma otemon.JPG|{{ill|Nakamura Castle (Mutsu Province)|ja|中村城 (Mutsu Province )|12=draft|12=draft}} Otemon Gate (2013) File:Nakamura shrine 003.JPG|Entrance to Nakamura Shrine, featuring a horse statue associated with {{ill|Sōma Nomaoi|ja|相馬野馬追|12=draft|12=draft}} (2013) == See Also == * {{ill|Nakamura Castle (Mutsu Province)|ja|中村城 (Mutsu Province )|12=draft|12=draft}} * {{ill|Sōma Nomaoi|ja|相馬野馬追|12=draft|12=draft}} == External Links == * [https://soumanakamurajinja.com/ Sōma Nakamura Shrine Official Website] * [https://ameblo.jp/umatoayumu-soma/ Uma to Ayumu SOMA Blog] ==References== {{reflist}} {{神道 横}} {{Myōken Faith}} {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Sōma Nakamura Shrine]] [[Category:Shrines in Fukushima Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Sōma City]] [[Category:History of Sōma City]] [[Category:Edo-period architecture]] [[Category:Sōma clan]] [[Category:Sōma Shrine]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Fukushima Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties (buildings)]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Sōma, Fukushima]] [[Category:History of Sōma, Fukushima]] [[Category:Sōma Shrines]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:New]] }} [[sn:Sōma Nakamura Shrine]] [[ja:相馬中村神社]] [[en:Sōma Nakamura Shrine]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Sōma Nakamura Shrine}}

revision.comment: 46 revisions imported: Bot: import full history from ja:相馬中村神社 up to rev 102625679
revision.text:
{{出典の明記|date=2020年3月}} {{神社 |名称 = 相馬中村神社 |画像 = [[File:Somanakamura shrine.jpg|270px]]
拝殿 {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=270}} |所在地 = [[福島県]][[相馬市]]中村字北町140 |位置 = {{coord|37|47|49.65|N|140|54|48.45|E|region:JP-07_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=相馬中村神社}} |祭神 = [[天之御中主神]] |社格 = |創建 = [[1611年]]（[[慶長]]16年） |本殿 = [[流造]] |別名 = 妙見中村神社 |札所等 = |例祭 = 4月18日・19日 |神事 = [[相馬野馬追]]（7月23日 - 25日） |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Fukushima Prefecture|label=相馬中村神社|float=center|relief=1|width=200|caption=}} }} '''相馬中村神社'''（そうまなかむらじんじゃ）は、[[福島県]][[相馬市]]中村に立地する[[神社]]。別名は'''妙見中村神社'''（みょうけんなかむらじんじゃ）だが、'''中村神社'''と省略されることもある。祭神は[[天之御中主神]]（[[妙見菩薩]]）。[[相馬野馬追]]の出陣式はここで行われる{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.minyu-net.com/news/news/FM20211115-667668.php |title=相馬中村神社に大鳥居　本県沖地震で被害、中川さんが奉納へ|publisher=福島民友新聞|accessdate=2021-11-15}}。 == 概要 == 相馬中村神社の起源は、[[相馬氏]]の始祖である[[平将門]]が[[承平 (日本)|承平]]年間（[[931年]] - [[937年]]）に[[下総国]][[猿島郡]]に妙見社を建立したことに始まるといわれる。相馬氏の相馬郡下向に伴い建立された。[[戦国時代 (日本)|戦国時代]]の16世紀後半には、[[中村城 (陸奥国)|中村城]]が相馬氏の北の居城となり、[[相馬盛胤 (十五代当主)|相馬盛胤]]や[[相馬隆胤]]などが中村城主となった。 [[1600年]]の[[関ヶ原の戦い]]の結果として相馬氏は改易されたが、[[1611年]]に旧領への復帰を果たして[[相馬中村藩|中村藩]]を立てた。この時、中村藩の初代藩主となった[[相馬利胤]]（相馬氏第17代当主）が、[[1611年]]に中村城内の南西に相馬氏の守護社である妙見社を建立したのが、現在の相馬中村神社の起源である。 現在の社殿は寛永20年（[[1643年]]）に中村藩2代藩主[[相馬義胤 (相馬中村藩主)|相馬義胤]]（相馬氏第18代当主）により建立され、国の[[重要文化財]]に指定されている。 明治時代に入って廃仏毀釈により本尊の妙見菩薩が廃棄され、相馬中村神社と改称した。 また、中村城本丸跡には、[[戊辰戦争]]後の[[1880年]]に建立された[[相馬神社 (相馬市)|相馬神社]]がある。 宮司は初代田代信盛から始まって、代々田代家が世襲して29代を数える。 かつては馬を飼育する厩舎も立地し、中には[[バシケーン]]（[[2010年]][[中山大障害]]優勝）や[[ミリオンディスク]]（[[2009年]][[カペラステークス]]優勝）など元競走馬も複数繋養されていたが[https://news.netkeiba.com/?pid=news_view&no=72447 相馬中村神社で第2の馬生を歩み始めたバシケーン/美浦トレセンニュース] - netkeiba.com・2013年2月7日[https://news.netkeiba.com/?pid=column_view&cid=33318 相馬と南相馬で会った馬たち] - netkeiba.com 2016年3月26日、2019年頃に厩舎は解体されており、現在は飼育は行っていない[https://jinjyairoiro.jp/soumanakamurajinjya/ 野馬追の地・相馬中村神社に参拝|アクセスや御朱印など]。 [[1928年]]建立のコンクリート製の大鳥居があったが、[[2021年]]2月の[[福島県沖地震 (2021年)|福島県沖地震]]で損傷した。その後、2021年12月にステンレス製の大鳥居（高さ7.4m）が神社に寄進されることになった。さらに翌[[2022年]]3月にも再び[[福島県沖地震 (2022年)|福島県沖地震]]が発生、石灯篭が壊れたり橋が崩落したりした{{YouTube|zIiaqIkXXoY|相馬野馬追に向けて 2022年の地震で被害受けた相馬中村神社 修復進む}}。 == 文化財 == '''重要文化財''' *本殿・幣殿・拝殿（1棟） **附 宮殿（くうでん）1基 == 画像 == File:Soma otemon.JPG|相馬中村城大手門(2013年6月3日） File:Nakamura shrine 003.JPG|中村神社入口。相馬野馬追にちなむ馬の像がある(2013年6月3日） ==脚注== {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} ==関連項目== *[[中村城 (陸奥国)]] *[[相馬野馬追]] == 外部リンク == *[https://soumanakamurajinja.com/ 相馬中村神社　公式ページ] *[https://ameblo.jp/umatoayumu-soma/ 馬とあゆむSOMA　公式ブログ（中村神社内厩舎の運営団体の一つ）] {{神道 横}} {{DEFAULTSORT:そうまなかむらしんしや}} [[Category:福島県の神社]] [[Category:福島県の重要文化財]] [[Category:重要文化財 (建造物)]] [[Category:相馬市の建築物]] [[Category:相馬市の歴史]] [[Category:江戸時代の建築]] [[Category:相馬氏|社]] [[Category:相馬神社]]

revision.text:
{{infobox religious building | name = Sōma Nakamura Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Somanakamura shrine.jpg|270px]]
Haiden {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=270}} | location = 140 Nakamura, Kitamachi, Sōma City, Fukushima Prefecture, Japan | coordinates = {{coord|37|47|49.65|N|140|54|48.45|E|region:JP-07_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Sōma Nakamura Shrine}} | deity = [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]] | Shrine Rank = | founded = 1611 (Keichō 16) | main hall = [[Nagare-zukuri]] | Other name = Myōken Nakamura Shrine | festival = April 18–19 | rituals = [[Sōma Nomaoi]] (July 23–25) | map = {{location map|Japan Fukushima Prefecture|label=Sōma Nakamura Shrine|float=center|relief=1|width=200|caption=}} }} '''Sōma Nakamura Shrine''' (相馬中村神社) is a Shinto shrine located in Nakamura, Sōma City, Fukushima Prefecture. It is also known as '''Myōken Nakamura Shrine''' (妙見中村神社) or simply Nakamura Shrine. The enshrined deity is {{ill|Ame-no-Minakanushi|ja|天之御中主神|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (identified with {{ill|Myōken Bosatsu|ja|妙見菩薩|qq=draft|qq=draft}}). The shrine serves as the starting point for the {{ill|Sōma Nomaoi|ja|相馬野馬追|qq=draft|qq=draft}} festival.{{Cite web|url=https://www.minyu-net.com/news/news/FM20211115-667668.php |title=Sōma Nakamura Shrine to Receive New Grand Torii After Earthquake Damage |publisher=Fukushima Minyu Newspaper |accessdate=2021-11-15}} == Overview == The origins of Sōma Nakamura Shrine trace back to the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}} when {{ill|Taira no Masakado|ja|Taira no Masakado|1=Taira no Masakado|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the founder of the {{ill|Sōma clan|ja|相馬氏|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, established a Myōken Shrine in {{ill|Shimōsa Province|ja|下総国|qq=draft|qq=draft}} during the Jōhei era (931–937). As the Sōma clan moved to the Sōma district, a new shrine was established. By the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in the late 16th century, {{ill|Nakamura Castle (Mutsu Province)|ja|中村城 (Mutsu Province )|qq=draft|qq=draft}} became the clan's northern stronghold, governed by lords such as {{ill|Sōma Moritane|ja|相馬盛胤|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Sōma Takamine|ja|相馬隆胤|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. After the {{ill|Battle of Sekigahara|ja|関ヶ原の戦い|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in 1600, the Sōma clan was briefly dispossessed of their holdings but regained control in 1611, establishing the {{ill|Sōma Nakamura Domain|ja|相馬中村藩|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. The shrine was built by {{ill|Sōma Takatane|ja|相馬利胤|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the first lord of the Nakamura Domain, near the southwest of Nakamura Castle in 1611. The current main shrine building was constructed in {{ill|1643|ja|寛永20th year|qq=draft|qq=draft}} by {{ill|Sōma Yoshitane|ja|相馬義胤 (相馬中村藩主)|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the second domain lord, and has been designated as a {{ill|National Important Cultural Property|ja|重要文化財|1=Important Cultural Property (Japan)|2=ja|3=重要文化財|lt=Important Cultural Property|qq= (Japan)|4=ko|5=일본의_중요문화재|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. During the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離|qq=draft|qq=draft}} led to the removal of Myōken Bosatsu, and the shrine was renamed Sōma Nakamura Shrine. Another shrine, {{ill|Sōma Shrine (Sōma City)|lt=Sōma Shrine|ja|相馬神社 (相馬市)|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, was established in 1880 on the ruins of Nakamura Castle's main keep after the {{ill|Boshin War|ja|戊辰戦争|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. The shrine has been under the care of the Tashiro family, who have served as hereditary priests since its establishment. The shrine formerly maintained stables for horses, including retired racehorses such as {{ill|Basikeen|ja|バシケーン|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (winner of the 2010 Nakayama Grand Jump) and {{ill|Million Disc|ja|ミリオンディスク|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (winner of the 2009 Capella Stakes). However, the stables were dismantled around 2019.[https://news.netkeiba.com/?pid=news_view&no=72447 Basikeen Finds a New Home at Sōma Nakamura Shrine] == Cultural Properties == '''Important Cultural Properties''' * Main Shrine, Worship Hall, and Offering Hall (1 building) ''' Includes a "kuden" (宮殿) altar. == images == File:Soma otemon.JPG|{{ill|Nakamura Castle (Mutsu Province)|ja|中村城 (Mutsu Province )|qq=draft|qq=draft}} Otemon Gate (2013) File:Nakamura shrine 003.JPG|Entrance to Nakamura Shrine, featuring a horse statue associated with {{ill|Sōma Nomaoi|ja|相馬野馬追|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (2013) == See Also == * {{ill|Nakamura Castle (Mutsu Province)|ja|中村城 (Mutsu Province )|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Sōma Nomaoi|ja|相馬野馬追|qq=draft|qq=draft}} == External Links == * [https://soumanakamurajinja.com/ Sōma Nakamura Shrine Official Website] * [https://ameblo.jp/umatoayumu-soma/ Uma to Ayumu SOMA Blog] ==References== {{reflist}} {{神道 横}} {{Myōken Faith}} {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Sōma Nakamura Shrine]] [[Category:Shrines in Fukushima Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Sōma City]] [[Category:History of Sōma City]] [[Category:Edo-period architecture]] [[Category:Sōma clan]] [[Category:Sōma Shrine]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Fukushima Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties (buildings)]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Sōma, Fukushima]] [[Category:History of Sōma, Fukushima]] [[Category:Sōma Shrines]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:New]] }} [[sn:Sōma Nakamura Shrine]] [[ja:相馬中村神社]] [[en:Sōma Nakamura Shrine]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Sōma Nakamura Shrine}} {{translated page|ja|相馬中村神社|version=102625679|comment=Imported full ja history}}

During the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離|qq=draft|qq=draft}} led to the removal of Myōken Bosatsu, and the shrine was renamed Sōma Nakamura Shrine. The shrine has been under the care of the Tashiro family, who have served as hereditary priests since its establishment. The shrine formerly maintained stables for horses, including retired racehorses such as {{ill|Basikeen|ja|バシケーン|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (winner of the 2010 Nakayama Grand Jump) and {{ill|Million Disc|ja|ミリオンディスク|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (winner of the 2009 Capella Stakes). However, the stables were dismantled around 2019.[https://news.netkeiba.com/?pid=news_view&no=72447 Basikeen Finds a New Home at Sōma Nakamura Shrine] == Cultural Properties == '''Important Cultural Properties''' * Main Shrine, Worship Hall, and Offering Hall (1 building) ''' Includes a "kuden" (宮殿) altar. == images == File:Soma otemon.JPG|{{ill|Nakamura Castle (Mutsu Province)|ja|中村城 (Mutsu Province )|qq=draft|qq=draft}} Otemon Gate (2013) File:Nakamura shrine 003.JPG|Entrance to Nakamura Shrine, featuring a horse statue associated with {{ill|Sōma Nomaoi|ja|相馬野馬追|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (2013) == See Also == * {{ill|Nakamura Castle (Mutsu Province)|ja|中村城 (Mutsu Province )|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Sōma Nomaoi|ja|相馬野馬追|qq=draft|qq=draft}} == External Links == * [https://soumanakamurajinja.com/ Sōma Nakamura Shrine Official Website] * [https://ameblo.jp/umatoayumu-soma/ Uma to Ayumu SOMA Blog] ==References== {{reflist}} {{神道 横}} {{Myōken Faith}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Sōma Nakamura Shrine}} {{translated page|ja|相馬中村神社|version=102625679|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Sōma Nakamura Shrine]] [[Category:Shrines in Fukushima Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Sōma City]] [[Category:History of Sōma City]] [[Category:Edo-period architecture]] [[Category:Sōma clan]] [[Category:Sōma Shrine]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Fukushima Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties (buildings)]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Sōma, Fukushima]] [[Category:History of Sōma, Fukushima]] [[Category:Sōma Shrines]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:New]] }} [[sn:Sōma Nakamura Shrine]] [[ja:相馬中村神社]] [[en:Sōma Nakamura Shrine]]
